Do not attempt to modify the jetski. Modifications may reduce safety and reliability and could make the watercraft unsafe or illegal to operate.

7. Watch your surroundings at all times
Scan carefully for swimmers and stay away from swimming areas. Swimmers can be difficult to see, and you could accidentally hit someone in the water.

Maintain a safe distance from other boats and watercraft. Also watch for ski ropes, fishing lines, and other hazards. Follow local boating rules and always check behind you before making a turn.

<PIC>

In short: wear the proper gear, stay sober, complete your checks, keep feet in the footwell, avoid unsafe passengers or conditions, do not modify the watercraft, and operate defensively with constant awareness of swimmers, boats, and obstacles.,['Manual20_19', 'Manual20_20', 'Manual20_21', 'Manual20_22']

Your jetski has several important watercraft characteristics you should understand before riding:

1. Steering depends on jet thrust

Your jetski is steered by jet thrust, not by a rudder. When you turn the handlebars, the jet thrust nozzle changes direction, and the watercraft turns according to the amount of thrust being produced.

This means the throttle is very important for steering:

- With throttle applied, the jet pump produces thrust and the watercraft responds to handlebar input.
- If you release the throttle completely, only minimum thrust is produced.
- At speeds above trolling speed, your ability to steer will decrease rapidly without throttle.
- You may still have slight turning ability immediately after releasing the throttle, but once the engine slows down, the watercraft will not respond effectively to the handlebars until you apply throttle again or slow to trolling speed.

For safety, practice turning in a wide, open area without obstacles until you are comfortable with how the watercraft responds.

2. Always shut the engine off before clearing the jet intake

If weeds, debris, or other objects collect around the jet intake, stop the engine first. Then remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch before attempting to clear anything from the intake area. This helps prevent accidental engine operation while you are near moving parts.

<PIC>

In the image above, the clip and engine shut-off switch are shown. Removing the clip disables the engine and helps prevent accidental starting while you inspect or clear the intake area.

3. The watercraft is water-jet propelled and has no neutral

This jetski uses a water-jet propulsion system. The jet pump is directly connected to the engine, so whenever the engine is running, some jet thrust can be produced. Because of this, the watercraft may move whenever the engine is on.

Important: there is no true “neutral” position.

4. Keep clear of the intake grate while the engine is running

Stay away from the intake grate whenever the engine is on. Long hair, loose clothing, straps from a personal flotation device, or similar items can be drawn into moving parts. This can cause severe injury or drowning.

5. Never put objects into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running

Do not insert hands, tools, ropes, or any other object into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running. Contact with rotating jet pump components can result in severe injury or death.

<PIC>

The image above shows the intake grate and jet thrust nozzle locations. These are the key areas to avoid while the engine is running, and they should only be inspected or cleared after the engine is stopped and the shut-off clip is removed.,['Manual20_24', 'Manual20_25']

Your jetski is legally considered a powerboat, so whenever you encounter another vessel you must follow the waterway “Rules of the Road” and any local regulations for the area you are riding in.

Key right-of-way terms:
- Stand-on vessel: the vessel with the right-of-way. It should normally keep its course and speed so the other vessel can predict its movement.
- Give-way vessel: the vessel that must take early, clear, and positive action to stay out of the way.
- If a collision appears unavoidable, neither vessel has the right-of-way. Both operators must do everything necessary to avoid the collision.

<PIC>

As shown above, when another vessel is in the white sector ahead or to your starboard/right side, you generally must give way. Vessels in the shaded sector generally must yield to you, but you should still operate defensively and be ready to avoid a collision.

1. Meeting head-on

<PIC>

If you and another power-driven vessel are approaching each other head-on and there is a risk of collision, neither vessel has the right-of-way. Both vessels should alter course to avoid each other. In normal practice, turn so that you keep the other vessel on your port/left side as you pass.

This rule does not apply if both vessels can safely pass without changing course or speed.

2. Crossing another vessel’s path

<PIC>

When two power-driven vessels are crossing close enough that a collision could occur:
- If the other vessel is on your starboard/right side, you are the give-way vessel. Slow down, change course, and pass behind it when safe.
- If the other vessel is on your port/left side, you are normally the stand-on vessel. Maintain your course and speed, provided the other vessel is giving way as it should.

Even when you are the stand-on vessel, stay alert. If the other operator does not take proper action, you must act to avoid a collision.

3. Overtaking or being overtaken

If you are passing another vessel, you are always the give-way vessel. Keep clear of the vessel you are overtaking until you are well past and safely clear.

If another vessel is passing you, maintain your course and speed so the other operator can steer around you safely.

4. Narrow channels, bends, fishing vessels, and sailing vessels

- In narrow channels, keep to the right when it is safe and practical.
- When approaching a bend where your view is blocked, proceed with extra caution. A sound-signaling device, such as a whistle or horn, should be available for signaling other vessels.
- Fishing vessels using nets, lines, or trawls generally have the right-of-way, regardless of position. However, they should not block safe passage in narrow channels.
- Sailing vessels should normally be given the right-of-way. Exceptions include when the sailboat is overtaking you, when it must keep clear of a fishing vessel, or when it must not interfere with a power-driven vessel that can safely navigate only within a narrow channel.

5. Buoys and navigation markers

<PIC>

Follow the buoy and marker system used in the waters where you ride. In U.S. waters, a common rule is “red right returning”: when traveling from open water toward a harbor, keep red buoys on your starboard/right side. When leaving the harbor, this is reversed.

Markers can also indicate speed zones, restricted areas, danger areas, and general navigation information. Because markings may vary by location, check local boating rules before riding in unfamiliar waters.

Additional safe-riding practices:
- Scan constantly for boats, swimmers, objects, and changing water conditions.
- Keep a safe distance from all vessels and people.
- Do not follow directly behind boats or other watercraft.
- Avoid sharp or unpredictable turns near others.
- Check behind you before turning.
- Ride at a safe speed and take early action to avoid collisions.
- Remember that a jetski does not stop like a road vehicle, and you need throttle to steer effectively.,['Manual20_26', 'Manual20_27', 'Manual20_28', 'Manual20_29']

To remove and reinstall the seat on your WaveRunner, use the seat latch located at the rear of the seat.

<PIC>

To remove the seat:
1. Stand at the rear of the watercraft and locate the seat latch.
2. Pull the seat latch upward.
3. While holding the latch up, lift and pull the seat off the watercraft.

<PIC>

To install the seat:
1. Position the seat so the projection at the front of the seat lines up with the stay on the deck.
2. Insert the front projection fully into the stay.
3. Lower the rear of the seat into position.
4. Push down firmly on the rear of the seat until it locks securely in place.

<PIC>

Before operating the watercraft, always check that the seat is properly secured. Gently lift the rear of the seat to confirm it is locked and does not come loose.,['Manual20_35', 'Manual20_36', 'Manual20_37']

To open and close the hood during daily use, follow these steps:

1. **Open the hood**
   - Locate the hood latch at the front of the hood.
   - Push the latch **①** downward to release it.
   - While the latch is released, lift the hood upward to open it.

<PIC>

2. **Close the hood**
   - Lower the hood back into position.
   - Press down firmly on the hood until it locks securely in place.

<PIC>

Before operating the jetski, always check that the hood is fully latched and properly secured. This helps prevent the hood from opening unexpectedly while riding.,['Manual20_38', 'Manual20_39']

Yes—there are two different filler caps on the WaveRunner, and both are removed the same way: turn the cap counterclockwise to loosen and remove it.

1. Fuel tank filler cap
The fuel tank filler cap is located on the front deck area. To remove it, grip the cap marked ① in the image and turn it counterclockwise until it comes off.

<PIC>

2. Oil tank filler cap
The oil tank filler cap is located in the recessed area shown below. To remove it, turn the cap marked ① counterclockwise.

<PIC>

Before operating the watercraft, make sure both the fuel tank filler cap and the oil tank filler cap are tightened securely. This helps prevent fuel or oil leakage and keeps water from entering the tanks.,['Manual20_40', 'Manual20_41']

There are two different engine switches on the jetski, and they are used for different purposes:

1. Engine stop switch — normal engine stopping

Use the engine stop switch when you want to stop the engine during normal operation. It is the red button on the handlebar control. Simply push this red button to stop the engine normally.

<PIC>

2. Engine shut-off switch — safety shut-off / emergency stop

The engine shut-off switch works with the engine shut-off cord, also called the lanyard. Before starting the engine, attach the cord to your wrist and insert the clip at the end of the cord under the engine shut-off switch, which is the black button.

If the operator falls off the watercraft, the cord will pull the clip out of the switch, and the engine will stop automatically. This helps prevent the jetski from continuing to run without the operator.

<PIC>

Important safety points:

- Always attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist before starting the engine.
- Always make sure the clip is properly installed on the engine shut-off switch before starting.
- The engine will not start if the clip is removed from the engine shut-off switch.
- When the engine is not running, remove the clip from the shut-off switch to help prevent accidental starting or unauthorized use, especially by children or others.

<PIC>

In short: use the red engine stop switch for normal stopping, and use the black engine shut-off switch with the lanyard clip as the safety system that stops the engine automatically if the operator is separated from the jetski.,['Manual20_43', 'Manual20_44', 'Manual20_45']

There are two different levers on the jetski, and they are used for different purposes: the throttle lever controls engine speed while riding, and the choke lever helps start the engine when it is cold.

1. Throttle lever

The throttle lever is used to accelerate and slow down the jetski.

<PIC>

- Squeeze the throttle lever ① to increase engine speed and accelerate.
- Release the throttle lever to reduce engine speed.
- When fully released, the throttle returns to the idle position.

Important steering note: this jetski needs jet thrust to steer effectively. That means you normally need some throttle when turning. If you release the throttle completely while moving faster than trolling speed, steering ability will be greatly reduced until you apply throttle again or slow down to trolling speed.

2. Choke lever

The choke lever is mainly used when starting a cold engine. It enriches the air-fuel mixture to make cold starting easier.

<PIC>

- Move the choke lever ① in direction ⓐ to turn the choke ON when starting a cold engine.
- Move the lever in direction ⓑ to turn the choke OFF after the engine starts and begins running smoothly.

In short: use the throttle lever for speed control while operating the jetski, and use the choke lever only to assist with cold starts. Always remember that throttle input is also important for steering control on a jet-powered watercraft.,['Manual20_47', 'Manual20_46']

The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector is the trim control for your WaveRunner. It is located on the left handlebar grip and is used to adjust the watercraft’s trim angle while riding.

In simple terms, when you operate the QSTS selector, it changes the vertical angle of the jet thrust nozzle. This raises or lowers the bow of the watercraft, which can affect acceleration, top speed, handling, and ride attitude.

<PIC>

The QSTS has 5 selectable positions:

1. Two bow-down positions
2. Neutral “N”
3. Two bow-up positions

The neutral “N” position is suitable for most normal operating conditions. If you want to fine-tune performance for specific riding conditions, you can select either bow down or bow up.

<PIC>

To change the trim angle correctly:

1. Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.
2. Squeeze the shift lock lever.
3. Turn the QSTS selector to the desired position.
4. Release the shift lock lever so the selector locks securely in place.

Important: Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating the watercraft at full throttle. Doing so could damage the QSTS mechanism.

Bow-up setting:

Turn the grip to one of the bow-up positions when you want the bow to rise while the watercraft is on plane. This places less of the bow in the water, reducing water resistance. As a result, straight-line acceleration while on plane and top speed may improve.

However, in some water or load conditions, the watercraft may begin to “porpoise,” meaning the bow hops up and down on the water. If that happens, return the selector to neutral or choose a bow-down position for a more stable ride.

<PIC>

Bow-down setting is generally useful when you want the bow to sit lower in the water, which can help improve stability and reduce hopping in certain conditions. For everyday riding, start with neutral “N,” then adjust gradually based on water conditions, speed, passenger/cargo load, and handling feel.,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

The fuel meter and hour meter are both part of the multifunction information center on your WaveRunner, and they help you monitor fuel level and engine use while riding.

**Fuel meter**

The fuel meter lets you conveniently check the approximate amount of fuel remaining in the tank while you are operating the watercraft. It uses **8 display segments** to show the fuel level.

<PIC>

In general, more segments mean more fuel remaining. As fuel is used, the number of lit segments decreases. Because the reading can vary depending on riding conditions, fuel movement in the tank, water conditions, and the angle of the watercraft, it should be used as a **reference** rather than an exact measurement.

If the fuel level drops to about **13 L / 3.4 US gal / 2.9 Imp gal**, the lowest two fuel segments may blink as a low-fuel warning, along with the fuel warning indicator and warning light. Refuel as soon as possible if this happens.

**Hour meter**

The hour meter shows the total amount of time the engine has operated since the watercraft was new. It is mainly used to help track service and maintenance intervals.

<PIC>

For example, if the display shows **123.4**, that means the engine has accumulated 123.4 operating hours. This time is stored in the meter and will remain recorded even if the battery terminals are disconnected.

If the hour meter has a malfunction, the display may show **“_-_”**, and the warning light will blink.,['Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

Here’s how to inspect the fuel filter and fuel tank on your 2005 WaveRunner safely:

<PIC>

1. **Prepare the watercraft safely**
   - Stop the engine and make sure the watercraft is stable before opening any compartment.
   - Work in a well-ventilated area, away from flames, sparks, cigarettes, or other ignition sources.
   - Locate the fuel system components: the fuel filter and fuel tank are inside the watercraft, as shown in the component-location image above.

2. **Inspect the fuel filter**

<PIC>

- This watercraft uses a **one-piece, disposable fuel filter**.
- Check the filter area for signs of fuel leakage, damage, or water contamination.
- The fuel filter should be replaced:
  - After the initial **10 hours or first month** of operation, and
  - Every **200 hours or 24 months** thereafter, or
  - Any time **water is found in the filter**.

**Important safety warning:** Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself. If the filter is installed incorrectly, gasoline can leak, creating a serious fire or explosion hazard. If replacement is needed, have a Yamaha dealer replace it.

3. **Inspect the fuel tank**

<PIC>

- Check the fuel tank for any signs of **leakage**, fuel smell, cracks, or wet areas around the tank and fuel lines.
- Also check for signs of **water in the fuel system**. If water is present, or if the tank needs cleaning, have a Yamaha dealer service the watercraft.
- After checking, make sure the fuel tank filler cap is properly secured before operating the watercraft.

4. **Set the fuel cock correctly after inspection**
- When the engine is not running, always turn the fuel cock knob to **“OFF.”**
- When transporting or storing the watercraft, also keep the fuel cock knob in the **“OFF”** position to help prevent fuel leakage and reduce fire risk.,['Manual20_34', 'Manual20_81', 'Manual20_82']

The adjustable sponsons can be repositioned to fine-tune your jetski’s handling based on your riding preference and water conditions. They are located on both sides near the rear of the hull.

<PIC>

There are two main adjustment directions:

- **Up position:** Reduces water resistance. This helps improve straight-ahead acceleration once the watercraft is on plane and can also improve top speed.
- **Down position:** Gives the watercraft more “hook” in the water. This improves turning grip and turning performance.

<PIC>

To adjust the sponsons:

1. **Remove the mounting bolts on both sponsons.** Each sponson is secured with bolts that must be removed before repositioning.

<PIC>

2. **Remove both sponsons and reinstall them in the desired position.** Make sure the left and right sponsons are installed at the **same height/level**. Do not set one side higher or lower than the other, as this can affect handling balance.

3. **Tighten the bolts on both sponsons to the specified torque.**

<PIC>

**Tightening torque:** 18 N·m, which is approximately 1.8 kgf·m or 13 ft·lb.

After adjustment, check that all bolts are secure before operating the watercraft.,['Manual20_69', 'Manual20_70', 'Manual20_83', 'Manual20_84']

If weeds, grass, or debris get caught in the jet intake or around the impeller, the jet pump may cavitate. A common sign is that the engine speed rises, but the watercraft loses thrust or acceleration. If you keep operating it in this condition, the engine can overheat and may be damaged, so stop and inspect it as soon as you suspect a blockage.

<PIC>

Before cleaning, follow these safety steps carefully:

1. Stop the engine before beaching or handling the watercraft.
2. Remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch so the engine cannot start accidentally.
3. Keep hands, hair, clothing, straps, and tools away from the jet intake and jet thrust nozzle whenever the engine could run.

WARNING: Never try to remove weeds or debris from the jet intake, impeller, or jet thrust nozzle area while the engine is running. Contact with rotating jet pump parts can cause severe injury or death.

To clean the intake and impeller:

1. Beach the watercraft safely, then turn it onto its port/left side as shown below. Place a clean cloth, carpet, or other soft protective material under the hull to prevent scratches. Support the bow while turning it so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

2. Remove any weeds, grass, rope, plastic, or other debris from these areas:
   - Around the drive shaft
   - Around the impeller
   - Inside the pump housing
   - Around the jet thrust nozzle
   - Around the jet intake grate

3. After clearing the debris, check that the intake area is clean and that nothing remains wrapped around the drive shaft or impeller area.

If the debris is stuck, tightly wrapped, or difficult to remove, do not force it with tools that could damage the impeller or pump housing. In that case, have a Yamaha dealer inspect and service the watercraft.

To help prevent this from happening again, avoid riding in areas with heavy weed growth whenever possible. If you must travel through weedy areas, vary the throttle between partial and full throttle instead of running at a steady low speed or trolling speed, since weeds tend to accumulate more at steady or very slow speeds.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here’s how to start your jetski in the most common situations. Before starting in any situation, always make sure the engine shut-off cord is attached to your wrist and the clip is installed on the engine shut-off switch. Then press the green start switch to start the engine.

<PIC>

<PIC>

Important: The engine will not start if the clip is removed from the engine shut-off switch. Also, remove the clip whenever the engine is not running to help prevent accidental starting.

## 1. Starting and boarding in shallow water
1. Do not start the engine in water shallower than 60 cm / 2 ft. Move the watercraft into water deep enough before starting, then board from the side or rear.

<PIC>

2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Hold the handlebars with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and then proceed slowly.

## 2. Starting from a dock
1. Board the watercraft from the side.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock before starting off. Hold the handlebars with both hands, place both feet securely in the footwell, start the engine, and move away carefully.

<PIC>

## 3. Boarding alone before starting
1. Swim to the rear of the watercraft. Place both hands on the boarding platform, pull yourself up, and grasp the handgrip with one hand.

<PIC>

2. Pull yourself up into a kneeling position on the platform, then move forward onto the seat and sit astride.

<PIC>

3. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
4. Grip the handlebars firmly with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and look in all directions before moving off.

<PIC>

## 4. Boarding and starting with a passenger
1. The operator should board first and sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.
3. Have the passenger climb on board and sit on the seat behind the operator. Both the operator and passenger should help balance the watercraft during boarding.

<PIC>

4. Before starting, confirm that the passenger’s feet are on the floor of the footwell and that the passenger is holding securely onto the operator.
5. Start the engine, look carefully in all directions, and then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

## Safety reminders before starting off
- Always check for boats, swimmers, obstructions, and other hazards around you before accelerating.
- Do not apply throttle until any passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on securely.
- Keep clear of the jet thrust nozzle and intake area whenever the engine is running.
- Remember that jetskis do not have brakes. Steering depends on jet thrust, so if you release the throttle completely, steering response is greatly reduced.
- When avoiding an object, do not simply release the throttle; you need some throttle to steer effectively.
- Practice boarding and reboarding in shallow, safe water before riding in deeper areas.,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_71', 'Manual20_72', 'Manual20_73', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_92']

Here are the basic steps for boarding your jetski with one passenger in deep water. For safety, make sure both riders are wearing suitable PFDs and protective clothing, and do not exceed the total load limit of 160 kg (353 lb), including any cargo.

1. The operator boards first.
Swim to the rear of the watercraft, climb on board as you would when boarding alone, and sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.

2. Position the passenger at the rear.
Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft and prepare to climb on from the boarding platform area.

3. Have the passenger board and sit down.
The passenger should climb onto the rear platform carefully, move onto the seat, and sit behind the operator. While the passenger is boarding, both the operator and passenger should work together to keep the watercraft balanced.

<PIC>

4. Confirm the passenger is secure.
Before starting off, the operator should check that the passenger has both feet placed on the floor of the footwell and is securely holding on to the operator.

5. Start and move off safely.
Start the engine, look carefully in all directions for boats, swimmers, and obstructions, then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

Important safety reminder: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on to the operator. Before starting off, always confirm that the area around you is clear of boats, obstructions, and swimmers.

If the watercraft is difficult to balance while stopped, use this method instead:

1. Have the passenger steady the watercraft.
The passenger should remain in the water and hold the watercraft steady while the operator boards. The operator then pulls themselves up onto the platform, moves into a seated position, and balances the watercraft.

<PIC>

2. Start the engine at idle.
Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, install the clip on the engine shut-off switch, start the engine, and keep it at idle.

3. Have the passenger board as the craft begins moving.
The passenger should pull themselves up onto the platform, kneel down, and then crawl carefully onto the seat as the watercraft begins to accelerate.

<PIC>

4. Accelerate to planing speed.
Once both riders are seated securely, the operator can accelerate to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

<PIC>

Remember: This watercraft is designed for the operator and one passenger only. Do not operate it if the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and cargo exceeds 160 kg (353 lb).,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

If the jetski is hard to balance while stopped—especially when boarding with a passenger—use the following procedure to get underway more safely and smoothly:

1. Have the passenger steady the jetski first. While the passenger holds the watercraft steady from the water, the operator should pull themselves up onto the rear platform, move into a seated position, and then balance the jetski from the seat.

<PIC>

2. Once the operator is seated and stable, attach the engine shut-off lanyard to your left wrist, then install the lanyard clip onto the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle speed.

3. With the engine idling and the operator maintaining balance, the passenger should pull themselves up onto the rear platform, kneel first, and then carefully crawl forward onto the seat as the jetski begins to move and stabilize.

<PIC>

4. After the passenger is seated securely, with both feet in the footwell and holding on to the operator, the operator may accelerate to planing speed. Once the jetski is stable on plane, reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Do not apply throttle until the passenger is properly seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on securely.
- Before starting off, look carefully in all directions to make sure there are no boats, swimmers, or obstacles nearby.
- The heavier the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and cargo, the harder the jetski will be to balance at a standstill.
- Do not operate the jetski if the total load exceeds 160 kg (353 lb), including operator, passenger, and any cargo.,['jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

The maximum load capacity of this jetski is 160 kg (353 lb). This maximum load includes the total combined weight of:
  • the operator,
  • one passenger, and
  • any cargo or items carried on board.
This watercraft is designed for a maximum of 2 people total: 1 operator + 1 passenger. For safety and proper handling, do not exceed the 160 kg / 353 lb load limit, and do not allow more than two people to ride at the same time.,['Manual20_12']

Here is an overview of the base station for the XL490/XL495 landline phone models. XL490 base station The XL490 base station has a simple base layout with one main control button: • Find handset / Paging button: Press this button to make the handset ring so you can locate it if it has been misplaced nearby. • Registration mode: Press and hold this button to put the base station into registration mode, which is used when pairing an additional handset with the base station. XL495 base station The XL495 base station includes the same handset-finding and registration function, plus additional controls for the built-in answering machine: 1. Find handset / Paging button • Press to find handsets. • Press and hold to enter registration mode. 2. Speaker • Plays answering machine messages and related audio from the base station. 3. Answering machine on/off • Turns the answering machine on or off. 4. Delete • Deletes the current message during playback. • Press and hold to delete all old messages. 5. Volume down / Volume up • Decreases or increases the base station speaker volume. 6. Skip backward / Skip forward • Moves to the previous or next message during playback. 7. Play / Stop • Starts message playback. • Stops message playback. In short, the XL490 base station mainly provides charging, handset locating, and handset registration functions, while the XL495 base station adds dedicated answering machine controls and a speaker for message playback.,['Manual22_18', 'Manual22_19']

To connect the base station of your landline phone, please follow the steps below:

<PIC>

**Before you start:**
- Make sure the power supply voltage matches the voltage printed on the back or underside of the phone.
- Use only the supplied power adapter to avoid product damage or charging issues.

**1. Connect the power adapter**

Plug one end of the power adapter into the **DC input jack** located at the bottom of the base station, then plug the other end into a **wall power socket**.

<PIC>

**2. Connect the telephone line cord**

Plug one end of the line cord into the **telephone socket** at the bottom of the base station, then plug the other end into the **telephone wall socket**.

**Note:**
- If your telephone line is also used for DSL high-speed internet, install a **DSL filter** between the telephone line cord and the telephone wall socket. This helps prevent line noise and caller ID problems caused by DSL interference.
- In some countries, you may need to connect a line adapter to the line cord before plugging it into the telephone wall socket.
- The product information label is located on the bottom of the base station.,['Manual22_21', 'Manual22_22']

To install the handset, please follow these steps:

1. Before connecting or installing the handset, make sure the basic safety precautions are followed. Use only the supplied cables, power adapter, and approved batteries, and keep the charging contacts and battery area away from metal objects.

<PIC>

2. The batteries are already installed inside the handset. Before charging it for the first time, turn the handset over and pull the battery tape straight out from the battery door area.

<PIC>

3. After removing the battery tape, place the handset on the base station or charging cradle to begin charging. When it is seated correctly, you should hear a docking sound and the handset will start charging.

Warning: If you ever need to reinsert or replace the batteries, always check that the battery polarity (+/–) matches the markings inside the battery compartment. Incorrect polarity may damage the product.,['Manual22_20', 'Manual22_23']

To check the battery level of your landline handset, look at the battery icon on the handset display. This icon shows the current battery status.

<PIC>

You can understand the battery status as follows:

- Full / medium / low battery: the bars inside the battery icon show how much charge remains.
- When the handset is placed on the base station: the battery bars scroll or animate, which means the handset is charging.
- Empty battery icon flashing: the battery is low and the handset needs to be recharged.

<PIC>

Please note: if the batteries become completely empty, the handset will turn off. If you are on a call when the battery is almost empty, you may hear warning tones first, and the call may disconnect after the warning. To avoid interruption, place the handset back on the base station when the battery icon shows low or flashes.,['Manual22_28', 'Manual22_29']

If your handset stays in “Searching” status, it means the handset is not currently connected to the base station. Please try the following steps:

1. **Check that the base station has power**  
Make sure the power adapter is firmly connected to the DC input jack on the base station and plugged into a working wall socket. If the base station has no power, the handset will keep searching and cannot connect.

<PIC>

2. **Register the handset to the base station again**  
If the base station has power but the handset still shows “Searching”, the handset may need to be registered to the base station. Press and hold the paging/registration button on the base station to enter registration mode, then complete the handset registration from the handset menu if prompted.

<PIC>

3. **Move the handset closer to the base station**  
The handset may be out of range. Place it near the base station and wait a few moments to see whether the signal bars return. The more signal bars displayed, the better the connection between the handset and the base station.

If it still remains in “Searching” after these checks, please restart the base station by unplugging the power adapter for a short time, then plugging it back in and trying again.,['Manual22_22', 'Manual22_18']

You can identify the current phone status by checking the LED indicator on either the handset or the base station. The meaning depends on where the LED is located and whether it is steadily on, off, flashing, or flashing quickly.

### 1. Handset LED indicator
Before using the handset LED to show a specific type of status, you can choose what the LED should indicate:

**[Menu] > [Phone setup] > [LED status] > [Events status] / [Charge status]**, then press **[OK]** to confirm.

<PIC>

#### If you select **Events status**:
- **LED on:** Audio boost is enabled.
- **LED off:** The phone is in a call or in standby mode.
- **LED flashing:** One of the following events may apply:
  - There is a new answering machine message.
  - There is a new voicemail.
  - There is a missed call.
  - The phone is muted.

#### If you select **Charge status**:
- **LED on:**
  - The handset is correctly placed on the base or charging cradle in standby mode.
  - Audio boost is enabled during a call.
- **LED off:** The handset is not on the base or charging cradle.
- **LED flashing:** The phone is muted.

### 2. Base station LED indicator
You can also check the LED on the base station to understand answering machine, call, playback, memory, paging, or registration status.

<PIC>

#### Base station LED meanings:
- **Steady on:**
  - There is no new message and the memory is not full.
  - The handset is switched on.
  - The phone has entered paging mode.

- **Flashing:**
  - There is a new message and the memory is not full.
  - There is an incoming call.
  - The phone is recording an incoming message or announcement.
  - Messages are being played back.
  - Remote access is active, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

- **Flashing fast:**
  - There is no new message, but the memory is full.
  - The phone has entered registration mode.

In short, check the handset LED for events such as missed calls, voicemail, mute, audio boost, or charging status; check the base station LED for answering machine, message memory, incoming call, paging, playback, remote access, or registration status.,['Manual22_40', 'Manual22_46']

You can identify the current status of the landline base station by checking the LED indicator behavior on the base station. The LED may stay steadily on, flash, or flash quickly, and each pattern indicates a different operating condition.

<PIC>

**1. LED steady on**
This usually means the base station is in a normal or active ready state. It may indicate that:
- There are no new messages, and the answering machine memory is not full.
- The handset is switched on.
- The base station has entered paging mode, for example when you are using the base to locate the handset.

**2. LED flashing**
A flashing LED means the base station is currently handling a message, call, or answering-machine activity. It may indicate that:
- There is a new message, and the memory is not full.
- There is an incoming call.
- The phone is recording an incoming message or announcement.
- A message is being played back.
- Remote access is active, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

**3. LED flashing quickly**
A fast flashing LED usually indicates that the base station requires attention or is in a setup-related mode. It may indicate that:
- There are no new messages, but the answering machine memory is full.
- The base station has entered registration mode, such as when registering an additional handset.

So, in short: **steady on = normal/ready status**, **flashing = message, call, or playback activity**, and **fast flashing = memory full or registration mode**.,['Manual22_46']

To lower the roll bar on the lawn mower, please follow the steps below. For safety, lower it only when it is absolutely necessary, such as when you need temporary overhead clearance. Important safety notes before lowering the roll bar:
  • There is no rollover protection when the roll bar is in the down position.
  • Do not wear the seat belt while the roll bar is down.
  • Drive slowly and carefully with the roll bar lowered.
  • Raise and lock the roll bar again as soon as clearance allows.
  • Before driving under anything, check carefully for overhead obstacles such as branches, doorways, and electrical wires, and do not let the machine contact them.
Steps to lower the roll bar:
  1. Park the machine safely on a level surface, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.
  2. Remove the hairpin cotters, then remove the 2 pins that secure the roll bar.
  3. Lower the roll bar to the desired down position. There are 2 available down positions, as shown below.
  1. Reinstall the 2 pins through the roll bar brackets.
  2. Secure both pins with the hairpin cotters so the roll bar is properly held in the lowered position.
After passing through the low-clearance area, raise the roll bar back to the fully upright position, reinstall the pins, and secure them with the hairpin cotters. When the roll bar is fully raised and locked, use the seat belt.,['Manual23_32', 'Manual23_33']

For machines equipped with the suspension system, you can adjust the 2 rear-shock assemblies to change the ride feel and improve operator comfort. Set the suspension to the position that feels most comfortable for your operating conditions.

The rear-shock mounting slots include detent positions as reference points. These detents help you match both sides easily, but the shock assemblies can be positioned anywhere along the slot—not only directly in the detent positions.

<PIC>

In the adjustment scale shown above:
1. Firmest position — provides the stiffest ride.
2. Softest position — provides the softest ride.
3. Detents in the slots — reference positions for matching both sides.

Important: Always adjust the left and right rear-shock assemblies to the same position. Uneven left/right settings can affect ride quality and machine balance.

To adjust the rear-shock assemblies:

<PIC>

1. Flip the lever of the quick-release pin downward to the unlocked position.
2. Slide the top of the shock assembly left or right along the slot to select your preferred ride stiffness.
   - Move toward the firm position for a stiffer ride.
   - Move toward the soft position for a softer, more cushioned ride.
3. Repeat the adjustment on the opposite side, making sure the left and right shock assemblies are aligned symmetrically at the same position or matching detent.
4. Flip the quick-release pin lever upward to the locked position to secure the shock assembly.
5. Before operating, confirm that both quick-release pins are fully locked and both shock assemblies are set evenly.,['Manual23_37', 'Manual23_38']

For a mower equipped with an electric deck lift, adjust the height of cut as follows:

1. Raise the mower deck by pushing up on the deck-lift switch. The switch controls the deck movement: push up to raise the deck and push down to lower it.

<PIC>

2. Set the desired cutting height by moving the height-of-cut pin. Choose the hole in the height-of-cut bracket that matches the cutting height you want, then insert the pin securely into that hole.

<PIC>

3. Lower the deck into position by pushing down on the deck-lift switch. The deck will settle onto the selected height-of-cut setting.

<PIC>

Note: Each time you change the height of cut, also adjust the anti-scalp rollers to match the new cutting height. This helps prevent scalping the turf, especially on uneven ground.,['Manual23_55', 'Manual23_56', 'Manual23_55']

To load the lawn mower onto a trailer or truck safely, please follow these steps:

1. Choose the right trailer or truck
- Use a heavy-duty trailer or truck that is suitable for the mower’s weight and size.
- Use a full-width ramp only. Do not use two separate individual ramps, as they can increase the risk of the mower slipping or tipping.
- Make sure the trailer or truck has the required brakes, lights, and markings for transport.

2. Check the ramp angle and length
- The ramp angle must not exceed 15°.
- The ramp should be at least 4 times as long as the height from the ground to the trailer or truck bed.
- This helps keep the loading angle low and reduces the chance of a tip-over.

<PIC>

3. Prepare the trailer
- If using a trailer, connect it securely to the towing vehicle.
- Attach the safety chains.
- If equipped, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
- Lower the ramp and confirm again that the ramp-to-ground angle is no more than 15°.

4. Load the mower correctly
- Use extreme caution when driving on the ramp.
- Back the mower up the ramp onto the trailer or truck.
- When unloading, drive the mower forward down the ramp.
- Avoid sudden acceleration, sudden braking, or sharp steering while on the ramp, as this may cause loss of control or a tip-over.

<PIC>

5. Secure the mower after loading
- Once the mower is fully on the trailer or truck, shut off the engine.
- Remove the key.
- Engage the parking brake.
- Tie down the mower using straps, chains, cables, or ropes.
- Secure it near the front caster wheels and the rear bumper, using the tie-down loops shown below.
- Follow local tie-down requirements when transporting the machine.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Do not drive the mower on public streets or roadways.
- Always use a full-width ramp.
- Keep the ramp angle at 15° or less.
- Back up the ramp to load, and drive forward down the ramp to unload.
- Move slowly and smoothly while on the ramp.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower safely from a trailer or truck, follow these steps:

1. Lower the full-width ramp and check the ramp angle.  
   Make sure the angle between the ramp and the ground does not exceed 15 degrees. The ramp should be long enough to keep the slope gentle; as a guide, the ramp length should be at least 4 times the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground.

<PIC>

2. Drive the machine forward down the ramp.  
   Keep the machine straight on the ramp and move slowly and steadily until it is completely off the trailer or truck.

<PIC>

Warning: Use extreme caution when operating the machine on a ramp. Do not accelerate, brake, or change direction suddenly, as this could cause loss of control or a tip-over. Always use a full-width ramp rather than separate narrow ramps for each side of the mower.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

Change the engine oil every 100 hours, or more often if the mower is used in dirty or dusty conditions.

Before you begin, make sure you have the correct detergent engine oil, a drain pan, rags, and the proper wrenches. Used oil should be taken to a recycling center.

1. Warm the oil
Start the engine and let it run for about 5 minutes. Warm oil drains more completely.

2. Position the mower for draining
Park the machine so that the oil-drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side. This helps the crankcase drain fully.

3. Secure the mower
Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.

4. Drain the engine oil
Place a drain pan under the oil-drain hose, then follow the procedure for your engine type.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>
Pull the drain hose out from the frame, route the hose into a drain pan, loosen and remove the drain plug, and allow the oil to drain completely. After the oil has drained, reinstall the drain plug and tighten it securely.

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>
Use the same drain-hose method: pull the hose out, position it over a drain pan, loosen and remove the plug, drain the oil fully, then reinstall and tighten the plug securely.

5. Refill with new oil
Slowly pour about 80% of the specified oil amount into the filler tube first. Wait a few minutes for the oil to settle, then add small amounts until the level reaches the Full mark. Do not overfill the crankcase.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>
Check the oil level with the dipstick and continue adding oil gradually until it reaches the Full mark. Once the level is correct, reinstall and tighten the oil-fill cap securely.

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>
When checking the oil level, insert the dipstick into the tube without screwing the cap down. Add oil gradually until the level reaches the Full mark, then reinstall and tighten the cap securely.

6. Final check
Start the engine, drive the mower to a flat, level area, shut it off, and check the oil level again. Add oil if needed, but keep the level at the Full mark—do not run the engine with too much or too little oil.

Oil requirements:
- Kawasaki engines: detergent oil, API service SF, SG, SH, SJ, or SL.
- Kohler engines: detergent oil, API service SJ or higher.

Important: Running the engine with an overfilled or underfilled crankcase can damage the engine.,['Manual23_75', 'Manual23_86', 'Manual23_76', 'Manual23_87']

To remove the lawn mower’s air-cleaner filters, first confirm which engine your machine has, as the procedure is slightly different for Kawasaki and Kohler engines.

Before you begin, always prepare the mower safely:

1. Park the machine on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO).
3. Engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.

### For Kawasaki engines

1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.

<PIC>

3. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
4. Gently slide the primary filter straight out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Avoid knocking the filter against the side of the air-cleaner body, as this can loosen dirt and allow debris into the intake area.
5. Remove the safety filter only if you are replacing it.

### For Kohler engines

1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the air-inlet cover off the air-cleaner body.
3. Clean the air-inlet screen and the cover.
4. Reinstall the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.

<PIC>

5. Release the latches on the air-cleaner cover.
6. Pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
7. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
8. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Avoid bumping the filter against the housing while removing it.

<PIC>

9. Remove the inner filter only if you intend to replace it.

### Important filter handling notes

- Do not attempt to clean the safety filter or inner filter. If it is dirty, replace both the inner/safety filter and the primary filter.
- Do not clean the primary filter. Replace it if it is dirty, bent, or damaged.
- To prevent engine damage, do not run the engine unless both filters and the air-cleaner cover are properly installed.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_82', 'Manual23_83']

To replace the mower belt, follow these steps carefully:

1. Park the mower on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.
3. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
4. Lower the mower deck to the 76 mm / 3 inch height-of-cut position.
5. Remove the belt covers. Press the retaining tab down, slide it out, and then slide the belt cover off the mower deck as shown below.

<PIC>

6. Insert a 3/8-inch ratchet into the square hole in the idler arm. Use the ratchet to relieve tension from the idler spring.
7. With the spring tension released, remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley.
8. Remove the belt guide from the spring-loaded idler arm.
9. Remove the old mower belt.
10. Route the new mower belt around the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine. Make sure the belt follows the same path shown in the diagram below.

<PIC>

In the belt-routing diagram:
- 1 = Spring
- 2 = Clutch pulley
- 3 = Mower belt
- 4 = Spring-loaded idler assembly
- 5 = 3/8-inch ratchet
- 6 = Square hole in the idler arm for the ratchet

11. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
12. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole again, reinstall the idler spring.

Note: Make sure both ends of the spring are fully seated in their anchor grooves. If the spring is not seated correctly, the belt may not maintain proper tension.

13. Reinstall the belt covers. Slide each cover back into position, press it down, and confirm that the tab locks securely into the slot on the mower deck.

<PIC>

Important: Replace the mower belt if it shows signs of wear, including squealing while rotating, mower blades slipping while cutting, frayed edges, burn marks, or cracks in the belt.,['Manual23_99', 'Manual23_100', 'Manual23_101']

For example, to have the light **turn on at 2:00 AM** and **turn off at 7:00 AM**, follow these steps:

Yes. On this over-the-range microwave, the Favorite Recipe feature lets you save one cooking instruction in memory so you can start that same cooking setting quickly later.

You can reheat food in an over-the-range microwave by using the built-in Reheat sensor feature. This option automatically detects humidity from the food and adjusts the heating time, so you do not need to manually set the cooking time or power level. The Reheat sensor has 3 preset food categories: Steps to reheat food: 1. Place the food in a microwave-safe container. 2. Cover it loosely with a microwave-safe lid, waxed paper, or vented microwave-safe plastic wrap. 3. Touch the Reheat pad. 4. Select the food category by pressing the matching number: - 1 for Casserole - 2 for Dinner Plate - 3 for Soup/Sauce 5. The display will show the selected food type once, then switch to SENSING. The microwave will automatically determine the remaining reheating time. For example, to reheat a casserole, follow the process shown below: When reheating is complete, the microwave will beep four times and END will appear on the display. Recommended reheating amounts: Tips for best sensor reheating results: 1. Food should be at normal storage temperature before reheating. 2. Make sure the glass turntable and the outside of the container are dry, as extra moisture can affect the sensor. 3. Always cover food loosely with microwave-safe plastic wrap, waxed paper, or a lid. Do not seal the cover tightly, because steam needs to escape. 4. Do not open the door or press Stop/Clear while the microwave is sensing. After sensing is complete, the microwave will beep twice and show the remaining heating time. At that point, you may open the door to stir, turn, or rearrange the food if needed. 5. Use only microwave-safe containers, such as ovenproof glass, microwave-safe china without metal trim, or suitable microwave-safe plastic or paper products.,['Manual24_29', 'Manual24_30', 'Manual24_31']

The grease filter on your over-the-range microwave should be removed and cleaned regularly—at least once a month, or more often if you cook frequently or notice grease buildup.

1. Remove the grease filter
Slide the filter to one side, pull it downward, then push it toward the opposite side. The filter will drop out from the underside of the microwave.

<PIC>

2. Clean the filter
Soak the grease filter in hot water with a mild detergent, then rinse it thoroughly. Shake off excess water and allow it to dry before reinstalling.

Avoid using ammonia or harsh/corrosive cleaners, as they can damage or discolor the aluminum filter. Dishwasher cleaning may also cause the aluminum to darken, so hand washing is recommended if you want to maintain its appearance.

<PIC>

3. Reinstall the filter
Once the filter is dry, slide it into the side slot, then push it upward and toward the center of the microwave until it locks securely in place.

<PIC>

Important: Do not operate the vent hood without the grease filter installed. Running it without the filter may increase the risk of personal injury or property damage.,['Manual24_47', 'Manual24_48', 'Manual24_49']

If your over-the-range microwave is vented indoors (recirculating), the charcoal filter should be replaced every 6 to 12 months, or more often if you cook frequently or notice lingering odors. Please note that the charcoal filter cannot be washed or reused—it must be replaced.

Follow these steps to replace it:

1. **Disconnect power first.**  
Unplug the microwave or turn off power at the main power supply/circuit breaker before starting.

2. **Remove the vent grill mounting screws.**  
Locate the two middle screws on the top vent grill and remove them.

<PIC>

3. **Remove the vent grill.**  
Gently tip the grill forward, then lift it out of position.

4. **Take out the old charcoal filter.**  
Pull the old filter out from the top opening of the microwave.

<PIC>

5. **Install the new charcoal filter.**  
Slide the new charcoal filter into place. Make sure it rests at the same angled position shown below so it seats correctly.

<PIC>

6. **Reinstall the vent grill.**  
Slide the bottom of the grill back into place first. Then push the top inward until it snaps into position. Reinstall the two mounting screws securely.

7. **Restore power and reset the clock.**  
Turn the power back on at the main power supply or plug the microwave back in, then set the clock if needed.

Once completed, the microwave is ready to use again with the new charcoal filter installed.,['Manual24_50', 'Manual24_51', 'Manual24_52']

The motherboard provides three PCI Express 3.0 x16 physical slots for PCI Express graphics cards and other PCIe expansion cards. Their actual operating bandwidth depends on how many VGA/PCIe cards are installed.

<PIC>

PCI Express 3.0 x16 slot operating modes:

1. Single VGA/PCIe card
- PCIe 3.0 x16_1 gray slot: runs at x16 mode
- PCIe 3.0 x16_2: not used
- PCIe 3.0 x16_3: not used

For best performance with only one graphics card, install it in the PCIe 3.0 x16_1 gray slot.

2. Dual VGA/PCIe cards
- PCIe 3.0 x16_1: runs at x8 mode
- PCIe 3.0 x16_2: runs at x8 mode
- PCIe 3.0 x16_3: not used

3. Triple VGA/PCIe cards
- PCIe 3.0 x16_1: runs at x8 mode
- PCIe 3.0 x16_2: runs at x8 mode
- PCIe 3.0 x16_3: runs at x4 mode

If you want to set or limit the PCIe link generation speed for slot 1 or slot 2 in BIOS, enter BIOS setup and go to:

Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration > PCIEx16_1 / PCIEx16_2 Link Speed

Available link speed options:
- Auto
- Gen1
- Gen2
- Gen3

Recommended setting: keep it on Auto unless you need to force a specific PCIe generation for compatibility or troubleshooting.

Additional notes:
- For a single graphics card, use the PCIe 3.0 x16_1 gray slot to obtain the best performance.
- When using multiple graphics cards, make sure the power supply provides sufficient wattage and the required PCIe power connectors.
- For dual or triple graphics card configurations, connect chassis fans to CHA_FAN1/CHA_FAN2/CHA_FAN3 to improve airflow and maintain a better thermal environment.,['Manual25_31']

Before adjusting any motherboard jumper, please shut down the computer completely and unplug the power cord from the power supply/wall outlet. To avoid static damage, it is also recommended to touch a grounded metal object or use an anti-static wrist strap before handling the motherboard.

There are two motherboard jumper settings involved:

### 1. Clear RTC RAM jumper — 2-pin CLRTC
The CLRTC header is used to clear the Real Time Clock (RTC) RAM in CMOS. This resets CMOS data such as the system date/time, BIOS setup parameters, and system password-related settings.

<PIC>

To clear the RTC RAM:

1. Turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Locate the 2-pin CLRTC header on the motherboard.
3. Use a metal object, such as a screwdriver, to short the two pins.
4. Reconnect the power cord and turn ON the computer.
5. During startup, hold down the <Del> key to enter BIOS setup, then reconfigure the necessary settings such as date, time, and boot options.

If the system still does not recover after the above steps, remove the onboard button-cell battery, short the two CLRTC pins again, then reinstall the battery after clearing the CMOS.

Note: If the system hangs because of overclocking, you usually do not need to clear the RTC. Shut down and restart the system; the BIOS can automatically reset the related parameters to default values through the CPU Parameter Recall function.

### 2. CPU Over Voltage jumper — 3-pin CPU_OV
The CPU_OV jumper is used to enable additional CPU voltage options for overclocking. This setting depends on the installed CPU and should only be changed if you understand the risks of CPU overvoltage and overclocking.

<PIC>

CPU_OV jumper settings:

- Pins 1–2: Disable — default CPU voltage setting.
- Pins 2–3: Enable — allows higher CPU voltage settings for overclocking.

To change the CPU_OV jumper, move the jumper cap from the default pins 1–2 position to pins 2–3 if you need additional CPU voltage options. To return to the default voltage behavior, move the jumper cap back to pins 1–2.

Please make sure the system is powered off and unplugged before moving the jumper cap. Incorrect jumper settings or excessive voltage may cause instability or hardware damage.,['Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

The rear panel connectors are the external ports on the back I/O area of the motherboard. Before connecting or removing devices, please shut down the PC and unplug the power cord if you are installing or changing hardware connections.

<PIC>

Below is the function of each numbered rear panel connector shown in the image:

1. **PS/2 Mouse/Keyboard combo port**  
   Connects a PS/2 mouse or PS/2 keyboard.

2. **DisplayPort**  
   Connects DisplayPort-compatible monitors or display devices.

3. **VGA port**  
   A 15-pin port for a VGA monitor or other VGA-compatible display devices.

4. **LAN (RJ-45) port**  
   Provides Gigabit Ethernet connection to a Local Area Network through a network hub, router, or switch.

<PIC>

The LAN port has two LED indicators: one for activity/link status and one for connection speed.

<PIC>

**LAN LED indications:**
- **Activity/Link LED**
  - Off: No link
  - Orange: Linked
  - Orange blinking: Data activity
  - Orange blinking then steady: Ready to wake up from S5 mode
- **Speed LED**
  - Off: 10 Mbps connection
  - Orange: 100 Mbps connection
  - Green: 1 Gbps connection

5. **Center/Subwoofer port — orange**  
   Connects the center speaker or subwoofer in multi-channel audio configurations.

6. **Rear Speaker Out port — black**  
   Connects rear speakers in 4.1-channel, 5.1-channel, or 7.1-channel audio setups.

7. **Line In port — light blue**  
   Connects external audio sources such as a tape player, CD player, DVD player, or other audio input devices.

8. **Line Out port — lime**  
   Connects headphones or front speakers. In 4.1, 5.1, and 7.1-channel configurations, this port works as **Front Speaker Out**.

9. **Microphone port — pink**  
   Connects a microphone.

For speaker setup, use the following audio-port configuration reference:

<PIC>

10. **Optical S/PDIF Out port**  
   Connects the PC to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF-compliant digital audio devices.

11. **USB 3.1 Type-A port**  
   Connects USB 3.1 Type-A devices.

12. **USB 3.1 Type-C port**  
   Connects USB 3.1 Type-C mobile devices or peripherals.

13. **USB 3.0 ports 3–6**  
   Connect USB 3.0 devices. For best speed and performance, USB 3.0 devices should be connected to USB 3.0 ports.

14. **DVI-D port**  
   Connects DVI-D compatible display devices. Please note that DVI-D cannot be converted to output an RGB signal for CRT displays and is not compatible with DVI-I.

15. **HDMI port**  
   Connects HDMI display devices. It is HDCP-compliant and supports playback of protected content such as HD DVD and Blu-ray.

16. **USB 2.0 ports 13 and 14**  
   Connect USB 2.0 or USB 1.1 devices.

Additional notes:
- USB 3.0 devices are recommended to be connected to USB 3.0 ports for faster and better performance.
- USB devices connected to USB 2.0 and USB 3.0 ports are controlled by the xHCI controller; some older USB devices may require firmware updates for better compatibility.
- Multi-display output support may vary depending on the operating environment and display interface used.,['Manual25_35', 'Manual25_36', 'Manual25_37', 'Manual25_38']

You can configure the motherboard’s onboard LED lighting in either of the following ways:

1. Through BIOS: Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration
2. Through the LED Control app in Ai Suite 3

There are two configurable onboard lighting areas: Model name LEDs and SupremeFX LED.

### 1. Model name LEDs
The Model name LEDs are located under the printed motherboard model name and are used to highlight the model name area.

<PIC>

To change the Model name LED setting in BIOS:

Go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Model Name LED Lighting.

Available options:
- Disabled: Turns off the Model name LEDs
- Still Mode: Keeps the LEDs constantly lit
- Breathing Mode: Makes the LEDs blink/fade intermittently

<PIC>

### 2. SupremeFX LED
The SupremeFX LED is located around the onboard audio area. It provides lighting effects and also visually outlines the separation between the audio components and the rest of the motherboard.

<PIC>

To change the SupremeFX LED setting in BIOS:

Go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > SupremeFX LED Lighting.

Available options:
- Disabled: Turns off the SupremeFX LED
- Still Mode: Keeps the LED constantly lit
- Breathing Mode: Makes the LED blink/fade intermittently
- Flowing Mode: Enables a flowing lighting effect
