### Important note about the Standby Power LED
The Standby Power LED is only a status indicator and is not used for lighting effects. It lights up when the system is powered on, in sleep mode, or in soft-off mode. If this LED is on, please shut down the system and unplug the power cable before removing or installing any motherboard component.,['Manual25_55', 'Manual25_56', 'Manual25_57', 'Manual25_58']

You can update the motherboard BIOS in several ways, including EZ Flash 3, EZ Update, or BIOS Updater. For most users, we recommend using EZ Flash 3 because it runs directly from the BIOS environment and does not rely on the operating system.

<PIC>

## Recommended method: Update BIOS with EZ Flash 3

Before starting, please prepare the latest BIOS file for your exact motherboard model. If you plan to update via USB, copy the BIOS file to a USB flash drive first.

### 1. Enter EZ Flash 3
1. Restart the computer and enter the BIOS setup screen.
2. Switch to **Advanced Mode** if needed.
3. Go to **Tool**.
4. Select **EZ Flash 3 Utility**, then press **Enter** to launch it.

### 2. Choose an update method
EZ Flash 3 allows you to update the BIOS either **by USB** or **by Internet**, as shown in the image above.

## Option A: Update via USB
Use this method if you have already downloaded the BIOS file.

1. Insert the USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file into a USB port.
2. In EZ Flash 3, select **by USB**.
3. Press **Tab** to switch to the **Drive** field.
4. Use the **Up/Down arrow keys** to select the USB flash drive, then press **Enter**.
5. Press **Tab** again to switch to the **Folder Info** field.
6. Use the **Up/Down arrow keys** to select the BIOS file.
7. Press **Enter** to start the BIOS update process.

## Option B: Update via Internet
Use this method if the system has a stable internet connection.

1. In EZ Flash 3, select **by Internet**.
2. Use the **Left/Right arrow keys** to choose the internet connection method.
3. Press **Enter**.
4. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the BIOS update.

### 3. Restart after the update
When the update is complete, reboot the system as prompted.

## Important precautions
- It is recommended to save a copy of the original motherboard BIOS file to a USB flash drive in case you need to restore it later.
- If updating by USB, the USB flash drive should be **FAT32 or FAT16 formatted** and use a **single partition**.
- Do **not** shut down, restart, or reset the system while the BIOS is updating, as this may cause the system to fail to boot.
- After the update, load the default BIOS settings for better compatibility and stability: go to the **Exit** menu and select **Load Optimized Defaults**.,['Manual25_61']

To create a RAID array on this motherboard, please follow the steps below. This motherboard supports RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 5, and RAID 10 through the onboard Intel chipset when using SATA drives connected to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s ports.

1. Connect the SATA drives to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors

Connect your Serial ATA hard disk drives or SATA SSDs to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors on the motherboard. Please make sure the drives you want to use for RAID are connected to the Intel SATA ports, not to other storage controllers.

<PIC>

Notes before starting:
• Back up any important data first, as creating RAID may erase data on the selected drives.
• Make sure the drives do not already contain existing RAID volumes.
• RAID 10 requires four drives.
• RAID 5 requires at least three drives.
• RAID 0 improves speed but does not provide data redundancy.
• RAID 1 focuses on data mirroring/backup.

2. Enter BIOS and change SATA mode to RAID

Restart the system and enter BIOS. Then go to:

Advanced > SATA Configuration > SATA Mode Selection > RAID

By default, the SATA mode is usually set to AHCI. If you want to create a SATA RAID array using these connectors, change SATA Mode Selection to RAID.

3. Open EZ Tuning Wizard

In BIOS, press F11 on your keyboard, or click EZ Tuning Wizard from the BIOS screen.

<PIC>

4. Enable RAID setup

In EZ Tuning Wizard, click RAID, then click Yes to enable RAID.

Please confirm again that:
• The drives are connected to Intel SATA connectors.
• The selected drives do not contain an existing RAID volume.

5. Select the available drives

The system will display the available storage drives. Confirm the drives you want to use, then click Next to continue.

<PIC>

6. Choose the RAID usage type

Select the storage function you want to use, then click Next.

<PIC>

You can choose one of the following categories:

• Easy Backup: used for redundancy/backup-oriented RAID configurations.
• Super Speed: used for performance-oriented RAID configurations.

7. Select the RAID level

If you choose Easy Backup, select either:
• Easy Backup (RAID 1)
• Easy Backup (RAID 10)

Then click Next.

<PIC>

Note: Easy Backup (RAID 10) is only available when four drives are connected.

If you choose Super Speed, select either:
• Super Speed (RAID 0)
• Super Speed (RAID 5)

Then click Next.

<PIC>

8. Confirm and create the RAID array

After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue the RAID setup.

9. Finish and restart

When the RAID setup is complete, click Yes to exit the setup, then click OK to restart the system.

After the restart, you can proceed with operating system installation or storage configuration as needed.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_71', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

You can install OS 7 on this motherboard by using a SATA optical disc drive together with a USB installation device. This method loads the required USB 3.0 driver from the support DVD, while the OS installation starts from the USB device.

### Method: Using SATA ODD & USB devices

#### Requirements
Please prepare the following items before starting:

- 1 × support DVD
- 1 × OS 7 installation source
- 1 × SATA ODD / optical disc drive
- 1 × USB device, such as a USB ODD or USB storage device

If you are using a USB storage device, it should have at least 8 GB capacity. We recommend formatting the USB storage device before use to avoid file or boot conflicts.

### Installation steps

1. Prepare the OS 7 installation source:
   - If using a USB ODD, insert the OS 7 installation DVD into the USB ODD.
   - If using a USB storage device, copy all files from the OS 7 installation DVD to the USB storage device on another working computer.

2. Connect the USB ODD or USB storage device to the 100 series platform.

3. Insert the support DVD into the SATA ODD connected to the 100 series platform.

4. Power on the system. During POST, press **F8** to open the boot device selection screen.

<PIC>

5. In the boot menu, select the USB ODD or USB storage device that contains the OS 7 installation files, then press **Enter** to boot from it.

6. During the installation startup process, the USB 3.0 driver will be loaded automatically from the support DVD in the SATA ODD.

<PIC>

7. When the screen shows **“Setup is starting...”**, it indicates that the USB 3.0 driver has been loaded correctly.

8. Continue following the on-screen instructions to complete the OS 7 installation.

Tip: If the USB device does not appear in the boot menu, please check whether the USB device is properly connected, try another USB port, and ensure USB support is enabled in BIOS.,['Manual25_87', 'Manual25_88']

To secure the motherboard to the chassis, first make sure the system is powered off and the power cord is unplugged. Then place the motherboard into the chassis in the correct orientation: the side with the external I/O ports should face the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

After aligning the motherboard with the chassis standoffs, install nine screws into the screw holes marked by the red circles in the image. Tighten each screw evenly until the motherboard is secure.

Important notes:
- Do not overtighten the screws, as this may damage the motherboard.
- Keep loose screws and metal objects away from the motherboard circuitry to prevent short circuits.
- Always disconnect the power cord before installing or removing the motherboard to avoid injury or component damage.,['Manual25_6']

To prepare the motherboard for installing the Central Processing Unit (CPU), please complete the following checks and safety precautions first:

<PIC>

1. Power off the system completely
- Unplug all power cables before installing the CPU.
- Make sure the ATX power supply is switched off, or disconnect the power cord from the power supply.

2. Confirm CPU socket compatibility
- This motherboard uses an LGA1151 CPU socket.
- Install only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket.
- Do not install CPUs designed for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets, as they are not compatible and may damage the socket or CPU.

3. Check the CPU socket condition before installation
- Before installing the CPU, confirm that the PnP cap is present on the CPU socket.
- Inspect the socket contacts carefully and make sure they are not bent or damaged.
- If the PnP cap is missing, or if you notice any damage to the PnP cap, socket contacts, or motherboard components, contact your retailer before proceeding.

4. Keep the PnP cap after CPU installation
- Do not discard the PnP cap after installing the CPU.
- Keep it safely, as it may be required if the motherboard needs to be returned for service.

5. Avoid installation-related damage
- Damage caused by incorrect CPU installation or removal, incorrect placement, or mishandling/loss/removal of the PnP cap may not be covered by warranty.

Also, before touching the motherboard or any components, follow these handling precautions:

<PIC>

- Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.
- Use a grounded wrist strap, or touch a safely grounded metal object such as the power supply case, to prevent static electricity damage.
- Hold components by their edges and avoid touching ICs, contacts, or circuitry.
- If you remove any component, place it on a grounded antistatic pad or in the antistatic bag it came with.
- Make sure the power supply is fully switched off or disconnected before installing or removing any component, as working with power connected may seriously damage the motherboard, peripherals, or components.,['Manual25_9', 'Manual25_5']

You can check the system memory information for this motherboard in two ways: by reviewing the motherboard’s memory specifications/slot layout, and by checking the installed memory details in BIOS.

1. Memory type and slot layout

This motherboard is equipped with four DDR4 DIMM memory slots. The slots are labeled as DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2, as shown below:

<PIC>

Please note:
- This motherboard supports DDR4 memory only.
- DDR, DDR2, and DDR3 modules are not compatible with DDR4 slots because the notch position is different.
- Do not install DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 memory modules into the DDR4 DIMM slots.

2. Supported memory specifications

The motherboard supports the following system memory configuration:
- 4 x DDR4 DIMM slots
- Maximum capacity: up to 64 GB
- Supported module capacity: 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB
- Memory type: unbuffered, non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs
- Supported frequency range: DDR4 2133 MHz up to DDR4 3400 MHz when overclocked

3. Recommended memory installation positions

For better compatibility and stability, we recommend installing the memory modules according to the slot population guide below:

<PIC>

Recommended configurations:
- 1 DIMM: Install one memory module in DIMM_A2 for better compatibility. The system will run in single-channel mode.
- 2 DIMMs: Install the two memory modules in DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2. This enables dual-channel mode and is the recommended setup for two modules.
- 4 DIMMs: Install memory modules in all four slots: DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. This uses two pairs of dual-channel memory.

Additional note: If different memory capacities are installed in Channel A and Channel B, the system will map the matched capacity for dual-channel operation, while any remaining capacity from the larger channel will operate in single-channel mode.

4. Checking installed memory in BIOS

You can also view the installed memory information directly in BIOS. Enter BIOS and go to Advanced Mode. The Main menu provides an overview of basic system information, including memory information.

<PIC>

For more detailed module information, go to:
Tool menu > SPD Information

This section displays the Serial Presence Detect information of the memory module installed in the selected slot. You can select the slot to check, including:
- DIMM_A1
- DIMM_B1
- DIMM_A2
- DIMM_B2

5. Important compatibility and stability reminders

- A DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to help protect the CPU.
- If you install 4 GB or more memory, a 64-bit operating system is recommended so the system can use the memory effectively.
- For best compatibility, install memory modules with the same CAS Latency.
- It is recommended to use memory modules from the same vendor and, ideally, the same version or data code.
- If using all four DIMM slots or running memory at overclocked frequencies, better memory cooling is recommended for system stability.
- The default memory operating frequency depends on the memory module’s SPD information. Some high-frequency memory may initially run at a lower default speed and may require frequency adjustment in BIOS if you want it to run at its rated speed.,['Manual25_18', 'Manual25_19', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector (10-1 pin COM) is an internal motherboard header used to add a traditional serial/COM port to the system.

To use it, connect the cable from a compatible serial port module to the 10-1 pin COM header on the motherboard, then install the module’s DB-9 serial port bracket into an available slot opening at the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

As shown above, the COM header provides the serial signal pins, including DCD, RXD, TXD, DTR, GND, DSR, RTS, CTS, and RI. Please align the connector correctly with Pin 1 when attaching the module cable to avoid incorrect installation.

Please note: the COM/serial port module is not included with the motherboard and needs to be purchased separately.

You can also enable or configure the serial port in BIOS:

1. Enter BIOS Setup.
2. Go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration.
3. Configure the following options:
   - Serial Port [On]: enables or disables the serial port. Options: [On] / [Off]
   - Change Settings [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4]: available only when Serial Port is set to [On]. It lets you select the serial port base address and IRQ. Options include:
     - [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4]
     - [IO=2F8h; IRQ=3]
     - [IO=3E8h; IRQ=4]
     - [IO=2E8h; IRQ=3]

In short, this connector is not a rear I/O port by itself; it requires a separate COM port bracket/module to provide the external DB-9 serial port connection.,['Manual25_39']

The **TPM connector (14-1 pin TPM)** on the motherboard is an internal header used to connect a compatible **Trusted Platform Module (TPM)**. A TPM helps provide hardware-based security by securely storing items such as encryption keys, digital certificates, passwords, and other sensitive data. It can also help improve network security, protect digital identities, and verify platform integrity.

<PIC>

In the diagram, the TPM header is shown on the motherboard layout, with the enlarged view displaying the **14-1 pin pinout**. The missing pin/key position helps ensure the TPM module is aligned correctly. When connecting a TPM module, make sure the module matches the motherboard’s TPM header type and pin layout, and align **Pin 1** correctly before installation.

Before installing or removing a TPM module, please power off the system and unplug the power cord to avoid damage to the motherboard or connected components.,['Manual25_40']

The Thermal sensor connector, labeled as the 2-pin T_SENSOR or T_SENSOR1 header on the motherboard, is designed for connecting a thermistor cable. Once connected, it lets the system monitor the temperature of important motherboard areas or nearby connected devices, such as components around the VRM, graphics card area, storage area, or other chassis locations where you place the sensor tip.

<PIC>

The 2-pin T_SENSOR header uses the following pin definition:
- PIN 1: SENSOR IN
- PIN 2: GND

Please note that the thermistor cable is purchased separately. After connecting the cable to the T_SENSOR header, place the sensor end on or near the component/location you want to monitor, then enter BIOS to check the reading.

You can view the detected temperature in BIOS from the Monitor menu. It will appear as T_Sensor Temperature. If no thermistor cable is connected, or if the sensor is not detected correctly, the value may show as N/A.

<PIC>

You can also use T_Sensor as a temperature source for fan control in BIOS. This is useful if you want certain fans to react to the temperature of a specific area instead of only following CPU temperature.

Available fan control source options include:
- Chassis Fan 1/2/3 Q-Fan Source: CPU, MotherBoard, VRM, PCH, T_Sensor, EXT Sensor 1/2/3
- Extension Fan 1/2/3 Q-Fan Source: CPU, MotherBoard, VRM, PCH, T_Sensor, EXT Sensor 1/2/3

For example, if you place the thermistor near the graphics card or storage area, you can set a chassis fan’s Q-Fan Source to T_Sensor so that the fan speed increases according to that local temperature.

Important precautions:
- Turn off the computer and unplug the power cord before connecting or removing the thermistor cable.
- Make sure you connect the cable to the correct 2-pin T_SENSOR header.
- Do not place jumper caps on sensor or fan connectors.
- Keep loose metal objects such as screws, clips, or staples away from motherboard connectors to avoid short circuits.,['Manual25_43', 'Manual25_83']

To set Quick Release (QR or QPR) on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. After the pressure cooking programme has finished, locate the quick release button on the top of the pressure cooking lid.
2. Press the quick release button down firmly until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once the button is locked in the Vent position, hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal and means the cooker is releasing pressure quickly.

<PIC>

Wait until all pressure has been released. You will know it is safe to open the lid when the float valve drops down into the lid and is no longer raised.

Important safety reminders:
- Keep hands, face, and any exposed skin away from the steam release valve while steam is releasing.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chilis, pasta, or congee.
- Do not use Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains. For these foods, allow the pressure to release naturally.
- Never try to remove or force open the lid while the float valve is still raised. The float valve must be fully down before opening the lid.,['multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

<PIC>

1. Hold the lid by the handle and align the lid’s open-position marker with the matching marker on the cooker base.
2. Lower the lid straight down onto the lid track, making sure it sits evenly on the base.
3. Turn the lid clockwise until the lid’s close-position marker lines up with the matching marker on the cooker base.

<PIC>

When the lid is correctly closed, it will automatically set to “Seal,” and you should hear a jingle confirming it is in the correct position.

Important safety notes:
- The pressure cooking lid can be removed during non-pressure cooking, but it will lock once the cooker becomes pressurized.
- Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is raised.
- Never force the lid open. Wait until the float valve has dropped fully before opening the cooker.,['Manual30_11', 'Manual30_13']

The float valve does not need to be manually “set” before cooking. It is a safety indicator that shows whether the cooker is pressurized or depressurized.

**1. What the float valve positions mean**

**Pressurized:** The float valve has popped up above the surface of the lid. This means pressure is still inside the cooker, and the lid must not be opened.

<PIC>

**Depressurized:** The float valve has dropped down, and the top of the valve is flush with the lid. This means the pressure has been released, and the lid can be opened safely if it turns easily.

<PIC>

**2. How to install the float valve**

If the float valve or silicone cap has been removed for cleaning, reinstall it before using the pressure cooking lid:

1. From the top of the lid, drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole.
2. Place one finger on the flat top of the float valve to hold it in place, then turn the lid over.

<PIC>

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly attach the silicone cap to the bottom of the float valve. Make sure it is secure so the float valve and cap can seal in steam properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

**Important safety notes:**

- Do not use the pressure cooker unless both the float valve and silicone cap are properly installed in the pressure cooking lid.
- Do not touch the float valve while the cooker is in use.
- Never try to open or force the lid open while the float valve is up. The float valve must be down before you attempt to remove the lid.,['Manual30_17', 'Manual30_18', 'Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The anti-block shield is a key safety part on the pressure cooking lid of your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Its job is to help prevent food particles, foam, or debris from entering the steam release pipe, which helps the cooker regulate pressure properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Why it matters:
- It helps keep the steam release pipe clear.
- It supports proper pressure regulation while cooking.
- It is required for safe pressure cooking.
- The pressure cooker should not be used without it installed.

Before using the pressure cooking lid, always check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and not clogged. This is especially important after cooking foods that foam, expand, or leave residue, such as rice, beans, pasta, oatmeal, soups, or stews. Proper cleaning and inspection before and after each use helps keep the cooker working safely and reliably.

To remove the anti-block shield:
1. Hold the lid firmly, similar to holding a steering wheel.
2. Place your thumbs against the side of the anti-block shield.
3. Press firmly toward the side of the lid and upward.
4. Continue pressing until the shield pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

To install the anti-block shield:
1. Position the anti-block shield over the prongs on the underside of the pressure cooking lid.
2. Press it down firmly.
3. Make sure it snaps securely into place before cooking.

<PIC>

Important: Do not attempt to pressure cook unless the anti-block shield is properly installed. If it is missing, loose, clogged, or damaged, clean or replace it before using the pressure cooking function.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_34', 'Manual30_35']

The steam release valve is controlled by the quick release button on the pressure cooking lid. This button determines whether the cooker is sealed for pressure cooking or venting to release pressure.

<PIC>

To set the steam release valve:

1. Seal position — for pressure cooking or natural release  
Leave the quick release button popped up. When the pressure cooking lid is closed, it automatically sets to the Seal position. In this position, pressure can build and be maintained inside the cooker.

2. Vent position — for quick release or venting  
Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. This opens the steam release valve so pressure can be released.

<PIC>

When the button is locked in the Vent position, hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during pressure release.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:

- Keep your hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve while steam is releasing.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve at any time.
- Do not turn the quick release button more than 1/4 inch or 45°. It should only be flicked lightly and allowed to spring back to its original position.
- Do not try to open the lid until all pressure has been released and the float valve has dropped.
- For foods that are thick, oily, foamy, high-starch, or expand during cooking, such as soups, stews, pasta, oatmeal, beans, or grains, use Natural Release unless your recipe specifically says otherwise.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set/install the anti-block shield on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the steam release pipe area and the small metal prongs where the anti-block shield attaches.
3. Place the anti-block shield directly over the prongs.
4. Press the shield down firmly until it snaps securely into position.

<PIC>

Before pressure cooking, always check that the anti-block shield is installed properly and is not clogged with food debris. Do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed.

The anti-block shield helps prevent food particles from entering the steam release pipe. This supports proper pressure regulation and is an important safety part required for pressure cooking.

<PIC>,['Manual30_35', 'Manual30_19']

To install the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. From the top of the pressure cooking lid, drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole. The flat end should remain visible on the top of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep one finger gently pressed on the flat top of the float valve so it does not fall out, then carefully turn the lid over.

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly attach the silicone cap to the bottom end of the float valve. Make sure the cap is fully seated and secure.

<PIC>

4. After installation, check that the float valve can move up and down freely. The float valve and silicone cap work together to seal in pressurized steam, so both parts must be correctly installed before pressure cooking.

Important safety notes:
- Do not use the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing, loose, or incorrectly installed.
- Do not touch the float valve while the cooker is in use.
- Do not try to open the lid while the float valve is raised. A raised float valve means the cooker is still pressurized. Wait until the float valve drops back down before opening the lid.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The condensation collector is installed on the back of the cooker base. Its purpose is to catch any liquid that may overflow from the condensation rim during cooking, so it should be fitted before you start cooking and emptied/rinsed after each use.

To install it:

1. Locate the tabs on the back of the cooker base.
2. Hold the condensation collector with its grooves facing the tabs.
3. Align the grooves on the condensation collector over the tabs on the cooker base.
4. Slide the condensation collector straight into place until it is securely seated.

<PIC>

Please note: When removing the condensation collector, pull it straight away from the cooker base. Do not pull it downward, as this may damage the tabs or collector.,['Manual30_38']

To install the silicone sealing ring on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Position the silicone sealing ring over the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.
3. Starting at one point, press the sealing ring down into place behind the rack.
4. Work your way around the full circle, pressing firmly and evenly so the ring sits flat with no puckering, twisting, or gaps.
5. Once installed, gently check all the way around the ring to make sure it is fully tucked behind the sealing ring rack.
6. Turn the lid over briefly to confirm the sealing ring stays in place. If installed correctly, it should fit snugly and should not fall out.

<PIC>

Helpful tip: The sealing ring is usually easiest to remove and install when it is wet and soapy.

Important safety checks before cooking:
- The sealing ring must be installed before using the cooker for pressure cooking.
- Install only one sealing ring in the lid at a time.
- Use only authorized sealing rings made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- Before each use, check that the sealing ring is clean, properly seated, and not puckered or loose.
- If steam escapes from the sides of the lid during pressure cooking, stop using the cooker and check that the sealing ring is correctly installed and in good condition.,['Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please follow these steps:

1. Make sure the cooker is unplugged and completely cooled to room temperature before handling the lid or removing any parts.

2. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.

3. Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring with your fingers, then gently pull it outward and away from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

4. Continue pulling around the ring until the entire silicone sealing ring comes free from the lid.

5. After removing it, check the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. It should be centered, securely attached, and at an even height all the way around the lid. If the rack looks bent, loose, or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

Tip: The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

Before using the cooker again, make sure the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and correctly installed. Do not use a stretched, cut, or damaged sealing ring.,['Manual30_30']

The sealing ring is a key part of the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. When the pressure cooking lid is closed, the sealing ring forms an airtight seal between the lid and the inner pot, allowing the cooker to build and maintain pressure safely.

<PIC>

Before pressure cooking, please make sure the sealing ring is installed correctly. Only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

A helpful tip: silicone can absorb strong aromas and flavours. If you often cook both sweet and savoury dishes, or strongly seasoned foods, keeping extra sealing rings can help reduce flavour transfer between meals.

Important safety notes:
- Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or otherwise damaged.
- Before every pressure-cooking use, check that the sealing ring is clean, properly seated, and free from cuts or deformation.
- Sealing rings naturally stretch over time. For best performance, replace the sealing ring every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, damage, or steam leaking from the sides of the lid.

Using a damaged or incorrectly installed sealing ring may cause food or steam to discharge, which can lead to injury or property damage.

To remove the sealing ring:
Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring and pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. After removing it, check that the rack is centred, secure, and evenly positioned all the way around.

<PIC>

To install the sealing ring:
Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack, then press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure it sits smoothly behind the rack without puckering or twisting. When installed correctly, the sealing ring should fit snugly and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If you notice steam escaping from the sides of the lid during use, turn the appliance off, allow it to depressurize safely, and check that the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and properly installed before using the cooker again.,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is a key safety and pressure-control part on the pressure cooking lid of your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It sits on the steam release pipe and allows steam to exit safely when pressure is being released.

<PIC>

Here are the main things to know:

1. How the steam release valve works
- The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe. This is normal.
- When the cooker releases pressure, steam comes out from the top of the steam release valve.
- The valve is required for pressure cooking and must be installed before use.
- It is an important safety component, so it should always be clean, correctly seated, and unobstructed.

WARNING: Do not cover, block, or obstruct the steam release valve in any way. Covering it with a cloth, towel, or any other object can create a safety risk.

2. How it is controlled during pressure release
The quick release button controls the steam release valve. It determines whether pressure is held inside the cooker or released.

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

When the button is pressed down into Vent, a strong jet of hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is expected and normal.

<PIC>

Important: Keep your hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve while steam is releasing. The steam is very hot and can cause burns.

3. Removing the steam release valve for cleaning or checking
To remove it, pull the steam release valve straight up and off the steam release pipe.

<PIC>

Before using the cooker again, make sure the steam release valve is reinstalled. Do not pressure cook without it in place.

4. Installing the steam release valve
To install it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly.

<PIC>

When installed correctly:
- It will still sit loosely on the steam release pipe; this is normal.
- It should remain in place when the lid is turned over.
- It should not be forced, modified, or blocked.

5. Safety checks before pressure cooking
Before using the pressure cooking lid, check that the following parts are clean and not clogged:
- Steam release valve
- Steam release pipe
- Anti-block shield
- Float valve

Also keep these safety points in mind:
- Do not lean over the steam release valve while the cooker is operating or while there is residual pressure.
- Do not place hands, face, or exposed skin over the valve during pressure release.
- Do not cover the valve with cloth, towels, or any other object.
- If steam releases in a steady stream from the steam release valve for longer than about 3 minutes during cooking, turn the appliance off and allow it to depressurize safely before checking the lid components.
- Always wait until the float valve has dropped before attempting to open the pressure cooking lid.,['Manual30_15', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is the control that operates the steam release valve on the pressure cooking lid. In simple terms, it determines whether the cooker is sealed to build/hold pressure, or venting to release pressure.

<PIC>

There are two key positions:

1. Seal position  
The button is popped up. This position keeps steam inside the cooker so pressure can build during pressure cooking.

2. Vent position  
The button is pressed down and locked. This opens the steam release valve so pressure can be released.

What’s helpful about this design is that it is easy to operate while keeping your hands farther away from the hot steam path.

A small operating note: although the lid may indicate “Turn to Seal,” the motion is more like a gentle flick rather than a full turn. Do not rotate the quick release button more than about 45°. After flicking it, let it spring back naturally to its original position.

For Quick Release after pressure cooking:

Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once it is in the Vent position, hot steam will release in a strong jet from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. Keep clear of the steam while it is venting. When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid; only then is it safe to open the lid.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:

- Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve while pressure is being released.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not try to open the lid while the float valve is still up.
- Avoid using Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, foamy, or high-starch foods such as stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, congee, beans, and grains. These foods can splatter or expand, so Natural Release is usually the safer option for them.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, is a pressure-release method used after pressure cooking. With Natural Release, cooking stops gradually: as the temperature inside the cooker lowers, the pressure decreases on its own over time.

<PIC>

What to do:
1. When the cooking program finishes, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position.
2. Turn Keep Warm off, unless your recipe specifically says otherwise.
3. Do not press the quick release button or try to vent steam manually.
4. Wait until the float valve drops down into the lid before opening.

What to expect:
- The food will continue to cook while the cooker naturally depressurizes. This is often called carryover cooking or resting.
- The depressurization time can vary depending on the amount and type of food inside, and it may take up to 40 minutes.
- Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop. Only then is it safe to remove the lid.

When to use Natural Release:
Natural Release is recommended for foods that may foam, splatter, thicken, or expand during cooking, such as:
- Soups, stews, and chilis
- Pasta, oatmeal, and congee
- Beans, grains, and similar expanding foods

Important safety reminder:
Do not attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up, and never force the lid open. If the float valve is raised, the cooker is still pressurized and must be allowed to release pressure fully before opening.,['Manual30_9']

The robot vacuum is made up of several key parts on the top, control panel, and underside. Here’s a clear breakdown of its anatomy:

### Top View
The top of the robot includes the main parts you’ll interact with when carrying the robot or removing the dust bin:

<PIC>

- **iAdapt® Localization Camera**: Helps the robot navigate and localize itself while cleaning.
- **Handle**: Allows you to lift and carry the robot easily.
- **Bin Release Button**: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying or maintenance.

### Buttons & Indicators
The control panel contains the main cleaning buttons and status indicators:

<PIC>

**Buttons:**
- **DOCK Button**: Sends the robot back to its charging base.
- **CLEAN Button**: Wakes the robot, starts a cleaning cycle, pauses cleaning, or resumes cleaning.
- **SPOT Button**: Starts spot cleaning for a concentrated area of debris.

**Indicators:**
- **Wi‑Fi® Indicator**: Shows Wi‑Fi connection status.
- **Troubleshooting Indicator**: Alerts you when the robot needs attention.
- **Battery Indicator**: Shows battery or charging status.
- **Full Bin Indicator**: Lets you know when the dust bin is full or needs attention.
- **Dirt Detect™ Indicator**: Lights when the robot detects a dirtier area and cleans it more thoroughly.

### Bottom View
The underside contains the main cleaning, movement, and charging components:

<PIC>

- **Debris Extractors**: The main rollers that lift and pull dirt, dust, and debris from the floor.
- **Extractor Caps**: End caps on the extractors that can be removed for cleaning hair or debris buildup.
- **Extractor Frame**: Holds the debris extractors in place.
- **Extractor Frame Release Tabs**: Used to open the extractor frame for maintenance.
- **Dust Bin**: Stores the debris collected during cleaning.
- **Side Brush**: Sweeps debris from edges and corners toward the extractors.
- **Charging Contacts**: Connect with the Home Base charging contacts to recharge the robot.
- **Floor Tracking Sensor**: Helps the robot track floor movement and navigate accurately while cleaning.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The vacuum can be tailored to different home cleaning needs using the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier. It offers two primary modes: Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode.

<PIC>

1. Halo Mode

Set the switch to the down position to activate Halo Mode. In this mode, the device creates an invisible circular protective zone that the vacuum will not enter. This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, or other objects you do not want the vacuum to bump into. The protected area extends approximately 24 inches from the center of the device.

<PIC>

2. Virtual Wall Mode

Set the switch to the up position to activate Virtual Wall Mode. In this mode, the device creates an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect. It can block openings up to 10 feet wide, such as doorways or room entrances, helping keep the vacuum out of areas you do not want cleaned.

<PIC>

Note: If you will not be using the Virtual Wall device for an extended period, switch it to the middle “Off” position to help preserve battery life.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

To empty the vacuum cleaner’s bin, please follow these steps:

1. **Pause the cleaning cycle if the vacuum is currently running.** This prevents debris from spilling while you remove the bin.

2. **Remove the dust bin.** Press the **bin release button** on the vacuum, then pull the bin out from the main body of the robot.
<PIC>

3. **Empty the bin into a trash can.** Open the bin door and hold the bin over your trash container so the collected dirt and debris can fall out completely.
<PIC>

4. **Close the bin door securely and reinstall the bin.** Make sure the bin clicks back into place before starting or resuming cleaning.

For best performance, empty the bin after each use and clean the filter regularly. If the **Full Bin** indicator turns on during a cleaning job, you can pause the job, empty the bin, reinstall it, and then continue cleaning. If the indicator stays on even though the bin does not look full, the full bin sensors may need to be wiped clean with a dry cloth.,['Manual32_8', 'Manual32_9']

To clean the vacuum cleaner filter, please follow these steps:

1. Remove the dust bin first, then open the filter compartment. Grasp the yellow tab on the filter and pull the filter out carefully.

<PIC>

2. Hold the filter over a trash container and gently tap it against the container to shake off loose dust and debris. Do not rinse the filter with water; just tap off the debris.

<PIC>

3. Reinstall the filter before closing the filter door. Make sure the yellow tab is facing upward when you insert it.

Important: The filter door will not close properly unless the filter is reinserted correctly.

For best performance, empty the bin and clean the filter after each use. The filter should be cleaned at least once per week, or twice per week if you have pets, and replaced every 2 months.,['Manual32_10', 'Manual32_11']

If the Full Bin indicator is on but the bin does not look full, the full bin sensors may be dusty and should be cleaned. Please follow these steps:

1. Remove and empty the dust bin.
Press the bin release button, take the bin out of the vacuum, and empty any debris before cleaning the sensors.
<PIC>

2. Clean the sensors inside the vacuum.
With the bin removed, locate the two full bin sensors inside the bin compartment. Gently wipe both sensors using a clean, dry cloth. Do not use water or cleaning solution.
<PIC>

3. Clean the sensor ports on the bin.
Wipe both the inner sensor ports and the outer sensor ports on the dust bin with a clean, dry cloth. Make sure no dust, hair, or debris is blocking the ports.
<PIC>

4. Reinstall the bin.
After all sensor areas are clean and dry, place the bin back into the vacuum securely.

Tip: For best performance, clean the full bin sensors about once every 2 weeks. Also, empty the bin after each use to help prevent false full-bin alerts.,['Manual32_12', 'Manual32_13', 'Manual32_14']

To clean the extractors (brushes/rollers), please follow the steps below. For safety, always disconnect the vacuum from power before cleaning or performing maintenance.

1. Turn the vacuum over and open the extractor frame. Pinch the yellow extractor frame release tabs, then lift up the extractor frame. Check the area and remove any visible obstructions.
<PIC>

2. Remove both extractors. Then remove the yellow extractor caps from the ends of the rollers. Clear away any hair, string, dust, or debris collected under the caps and around the metal axles. After cleaning, reinstall the extractor caps securely.
<PIC>

3. Clean the opposite ends of the extractors. Remove hair and debris from the square and hexagonal plastic pegs on the other side of each extractor, as buildup here can affect rotation.
<PIC>

4. Clear the vacuum path. With the extractors removed, check the opening inside the cleaning head and remove any debris blocking the airflow or roller area.
<PIC>

5. Reinstall the extractors. Make sure each extractor is placed back in the correct position by matching the color and shape of the extractor pegs with the matching icons on the cleaning head module. Close the extractor frame firmly once everything is seated correctly.

Recommended care frequency: clean the extractors once every 4 months, or once every 3 months if you have pets. For best cleaning performance, replace the extractors every 6–12 months.,['Manual32_19', 'Manual32_20', 'Manual32_21', 'Manual32_22']

To clean the sensors and charging contacts properly, please follow these steps:

1. **Power safety first**
   Before cleaning or maintenance, remove the vacuum from the Home Base/charger and make sure it is not charging. Handle the vacuum and charging dock only with dry hands.

2. **Clean the sensors**
   Turn the vacuum over and locate the sensors on the underside, including the sensors around the outer edge and the floor tracking sensor. Wipe each sensor gently with a **clean, dry cloth**.

   Do **not** spray cleaning solution, water, or any liquid directly onto the sensors or into the sensor openings, as moisture may damage the electronics.

   <PIC>

3. **Clean the charging contacts**
   Using a **clean, dry cloth**, wipe the charging contacts on the underside of the vacuum. Then wipe the matching charging contacts on the Home Base. Make sure both sets of contacts are free of dust, debris, or residue so the vacuum can charge reliably.

   <PIC>

For best performance, clean the sensors and charging contacts about **once per month**, or more often if you notice charging issues, navigation problems, or visible dust buildup.,['Manual32_17', 'Manual32_18']

To position the Home Base correctly, place it in an open, uncluttered area so the vacuum can easily find it, dock properly, and recharge without obstruction.

Please leave the following clearance around the Home Base:

- At least 1.5 ft / 0.5 m of open space on each side
- At least 4 ft / 1.2 m of open space in front
- At least 4 ft / 1.2 m away from stairs or drop-offs
- At least 8 ft / 2.4 m away from Virtual Wall barriers

<PIC>

Also make sure that:

- The Home Base stays plugged in at all times, so the vacuum can recharge whenever it returns.
- It is placed in an area with stable Wi-Fi coverage, allowing the vacuum to receive app commands, schedule updates, and status information.
- The floor in front of the Home Base is clear of cords, rugs with raised edges, small objects, or other obstacles that may interfere with docking.

Following these placement guidelines helps the vacuum return to the Home Base smoothly and maintain reliable charging and app connectivity.,['Manual32_18']

To clean the front caster wheel properly, please follow these steps. We recommend doing this about once per month, or more often if hair or debris builds up around the wheel.

1. Turn the vacuum over and locate the front caster wheel. Pull firmly upward on the front wheel assembly to remove it from the housing.

<PIC>

2. Check the wheel cavity and remove any dust, debris, hair, or threads from inside the opening.

3. Spin the wheel by hand to confirm it rotates freely. If the wheel feels stuck or does not spin smoothly, remove the wheel from its housing, then push firmly to take out the axle. Clear away any hair or debris wrapped around the axle or inside the wheel.

4. After cleaning, reinstall the axle, wheel, and housing parts. Press the front caster wheel assembly back into place until it clicks securely.

Once reinstalled, spin the wheel again briefly to make sure it moves smoothly before using the vacuum.,['Manual32_15']

To clean the side brush, please follow these steps:

1. Before maintenance, stop the vacuum and make sure it is not charging.

2. Turn the vacuum over to access the side brush. Use a coin or a small screwdriver to loosen and remove the screw in the center of the side brush, as shown below.

<PIC>

3. Lift off the side brush.

4. Remove any hair, thread, dust, or debris wrapped around the brush and the brush post.

5. Reinstall the side brush onto the brush post, then tighten the screw securely. Make sure the brush is seated properly and can rotate freely.

Recommended care frequency: clean the side brush once per month, or more often if hair or debris builds up quickly.,['Manual32_16']

The dual-mode virtual wall barrier helps control where the vacuum can and cannot clean. It creates an invisible boundary that the vacuum recognizes, so you can keep it in the areas you want cleaned and prevent it from entering areas you want to avoid. You can choose between two modes depending on your cleaning needs:

<PIC>

**1. Halo Mode — protects objects or small zones**  
Set the switch to the **down** position to use Halo Mode. In this mode, the device creates an invisible circular protection zone around itself that the vacuum will not enter. This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, cords, or any small area where you do not want the vacuum to go.

The protected “halo” extends approximately **24 inches from the center of the device**, creating about a **4-foot-wide protected area**.

<PIC>

**2. Virtual Wall Mode — blocks doorways or larger openings**  
Set the switch to the **up** position to use Virtual Wall Mode. In this mode, the device works like an invisible wall, preventing the vacuum from crossing into another room or area.

It can block openings up to approximately **10 feet** by projecting an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect. The barrier becomes wider the farther it extends from the device, making it suitable for doorways, hallways, or room entrances.

<PIC>

**Battery note:** Under normal use, the batteries typically last **8–10 months**. If you do not plan to use the virtual wall barrier for an extended period, switch it to the **middle “Off” position** to help preserve battery life.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum indicates a problem, it will alert you in three ways: a two-tone distress sound, a spoken error message, and a blinking Troubleshooting Indicator. You can identify the Troubleshooting Indicator on the control panel as the warning-triangle icon shown below.

<PIC>

Please try the following troubleshooting steps:

1. Listen to the spoken message
   The vacuum will announce what type of issue it has detected. Follow the message first if it gives a specific action, such as clearing an obstruction, emptying the bin, or checking the wheels/brushes.

2. Check for common physical issues
   - Remove any debris, cords, hair, or objects blocking the wheels, side brush, extractors, or vacuum path.
   - Make sure the dust bin is properly inserted and not full.
   - Wipe sensors and charging contacts with a clean, dry cloth if the issue may be related to navigation or charging.

3. Reboot the vacuum if the issue remains
   Some errors can be cleared by rebooting the vacuum:
   - Press and hold the CLEAN button for 10 seconds.
   - Keep holding until all indicators illuminate, then release.
   - After releasing CLEAN, you should hear a tone confirming the reboot was successful.

4. Check the app afterward
   If you use the scheduling feature, open the app after rebooting to confirm that your cleaning schedule is still set correctly.

If the warning continues after these steps, check the app for more detailed guidance, status information, and support videos specific to the error message.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera or equipment to a drop-ceiling T-rail, use the included drop-ceiling mounting hardware. This kit is designed to fit most 9/16", 15/16", and 1 1/2" T-rails.

1. Position the T-rail clips on the ceiling rail
Hold the two T-rail clips against the T-rail, then rotate each clip until it snaps securely onto the rail. Make sure both clips are seated evenly on the rail before continuing.

<PIC>

2. Set the correct clip spacing
Use the dashed guide lines on the mount plate template to set the proper distance between the two T-rail clips. The spacing should line up with the mount plate holes marked “G,” which are used for drop-ceiling T-rail mounting.

<PIC>

3. Secure the T-rail clips
Once the clips are correctly positioned, tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips using the included 5/64" / 2 mm hex key. This helps prevent the clips from shifting after installation.

<PIC>

4. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips
Align the mount plate with the clips, then fasten the mount plate through the holes marked “G.” After tightening, confirm that the mount plate is held firmly and sits flat against the clips.

<PIC>

5. Choose the correct screws for your T-rail type
For standard T-rails, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.

For recessed T-rails, use the recessed T-rail setup: install the rubber spacers between the mount plate and the T-rail clips, and use the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws. The black foam pads should be slightly compressed after installation, which indicates the assembly is seated properly.

<PIC>

Included drop-ceiling mounting hardware typically includes T-rail mounting clips with set screws, 6-32 x 4 mm screws for standard T-rails, 6-32 x 7 mm screws for recessed T-rails, rubber spacers, and a hex key.,['Manual33_10', 'Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_14']

To power the camera, connect it using Power over Ethernet (PoE):

1. Remove the cable guard from the camera so the Ethernet port and cable routing path are accessible.
2. Connect and route an Ethernet cable from an active port on an 802.3af PoE switch or from a PoE injector to the camera’s Ethernet port.
3. Make sure the cable is seated securely and routed cleanly so it will not be pinched when the camera is mounted or reassembled.

<PIC>

Note: Power over Ethernet supports a maximum Ethernet cable length of 300 ft / 100 m.,['Manual33_3']

The brake lever and parking brake button are used for two different purposes: the brake lever slows or stops the machine, while the parking brake button locks the brake when the machine is parked or before starting the engine.

1. Using the brake lever to slow down or stop

The machine is stopped by braking the entire drive system. To stop, release the throttle first, then squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar.

<PIC>

When slowing down or stopping, apply the brake gently and progressively—do not grab it suddenly. Sudden or excessive braking can cause the drive track to lose traction, especially on ice or hard-packed snow, which may reduce control and increase stopping distance. Always plan ahead and begin slowing early.

Also note: when the brake is applied, the brake light will illuminate.

2. Using the parking brake button

Use the parking brake when parking the machine or before starting the engine.

To apply the parking brake:
1. Squeeze the brake lever toward the handlebar.
2. While holding the lever, push down the parking brake button.
3. Release the brake lever so the button holds the brake in the locked position.

To release the parking brake:
- Simply squeeze the brake lever again. The parking brake will release.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Always set the parking brake before attempting to start the engine.
- Never drive or run the machine with the parking brake applied. Doing so can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.
- Before riding, confirm that the parking brake is fully released by squeezing the brake lever and checking that the machine can move normally.

3. Brake lever position caution

Make sure the end of the brake lever does not extend beyond the end of the handlebar. This helps prevent the brake lever from being damaged if the machine is placed on its side for service or inspection.

<PIC>

In short: release the throttle and squeeze the brake lever gently to slow or stop; use the parking brake button only to hold the machine stationary when parked or before starting, and always release it before riding.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40', 'Manual34_40']

Before using a snowmobile, complete the following preparation checks to help ensure the machine is safe, reliable, and ready to operate.

1. Review the safety labels and controls
Before starting, read all safety and warning labels on the snowmobile and make sure you understand the function of each operating control, including the throttle, brake, steering, engine stop switch, and parking brake. If any control or function is unclear, do not operate the machine until it has been checked or explained by a qualified dealer.
<PIC>

2. Perform the pre-operation inspection before starting the engine
Check each of the following items carefully:

- Fuel: Make sure there is enough fuel in the tank. After refueling, confirm that the fuel tank cap is closed securely. Keep snow or ice out of the tank when refueling.
<PIC>

- Engine oil: Confirm that the oil level is within the proper range and that there is sufficient oil in the oil tank before riding.
<PIC>

- Throttle lever: Press and release the throttle lever to make sure it moves smoothly and returns fully to the idle position when released.
<PIC>

- Manual starter: Inspect the starter rope for wear, damage, or fraying before use.
<PIC>

- Throttle Override System (T.O.R.S.): Check that the system operates properly.

- Brake: Confirm the brake operates correctly before starting or moving the snowmobile.

- V-belt and drive V-belt guard: Do not operate the engine if the drive belt or drive guard is not installed properly.

- Drive track: Inspect the track for damage, abnormal wear, or incorrect adjustment. Do not operate the snowmobile if the track is damaged.

- Slide runners, ski/ski runners, and steering system: Check for wear, damage, looseness, or excessive play. Steering should respond normally without abnormal movement.
<PIC>
<PIC>

- Lights: Verify that the headlight, tail light, and brake light work properly.

- Battery: Check that the battery is secure and in usable condition.

- Fittings and fasteners: Look over the machine for loose, missing, or damaged bolts, nuts, and other fasteners.

- Service tools and spare parts: Carry basic tools and useful spare parts such as a flashlight, tape, tow rope, emergency starter rope, spare drive belt, bulbs, and spark plugs. For longer rides, carry extra fuel and oil.

3. Set the parking brake before starting
Apply the parking brake before attempting to start the engine. Once the engine is running and you are ready to move, release the parking brake. Never ride with the parking brake applied, as it can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking ability.

4. Wear proper protective clothing
Always wear an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, suitable snowmobile clothing, boots, and gloves or mittens that still allow you to operate the controls safely.
<PIC>

5. Follow key safety precautions before riding
- Do not operate the snowmobile after drinking alcohol or taking drugs.
- Do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine while starting, inspecting, or adjusting it.
- Handle fuel carefully. Refuel outdoors, never while smoking, and never near an open flame. Wipe up spilled fuel immediately.
<PIC>
- Do not operate on public roads.
- Do not carry passengers if the model is designed for the operator only.
- Avoid running the engine indoors or in enclosed areas because exhaust fumes are dangerous.

Completing these checks before every ride only takes a short time, but it greatly improves safety and helps prevent breakdowns while operating the snowmobile.,['Manual34_7', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_107', 'Manual34_108', 'Manual34_11', 'Manual34_10']

Before starting the snowmobile engine, please complete the basic safety checks first:

- Apply the parking brake before attempting to start the engine.
- Make sure the engine stop switch is in the “ON” / run position.
- Check that the throttle, brake, and steering operate properly.
- Do not start or run the engine in an enclosed area unless you are only moving the machine in or out, and make sure outside doors are open because exhaust fumes are dangerous.

<PIC>

### 1. Set the starter lever / choke
Fully open the starter lever, also called the choke, before starting.

Use the starter lever positions as follows:

- **Fully open**: for starting a cold engine.
- **Half-open**: for warming up the engine after it starts.
- **Close**: for starting a warm engine, or after the engine has warmed up enough to run smoothly.

<PIC>

### 2. For a manual starting model
Follow these steps if your snowmobile uses a recoil starter:

1. Turn the main switch to the **“ON”** position.
2. Pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage.
3. Once engaged, pull the starter handle briskly to start the engine.
4. After the engine starts, move the starter lever / choke to the **“Half-open”** position.
5. Let the engine warm up.
6. When the engine can continue running smoothly without running roughly or stalling, return the starter lever to the **“Close”** position.

<PIC>

### 3. For an electric starting model
Follow these steps if your snowmobile has electric start:

1. Turn the main switch to the **“START”** position.
2. Release the switch immediately after the engine starts.
3. Move the starter lever / choke to the **“Half-open”** position.
4. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly.
5. When the engine does not run roughly or begin to stall, return the starter lever to the **“Close”** position.

<PIC>

### Important cautions
- If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and then try again.
- Keep each electric-start attempt as short as possible to preserve the battery.
- Do not crank the engine for more than **10 seconds** in any one attempt.
- Never ride the snowmobile with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake and reduce braking performance.
- After the engine is warmed up and before moving, release the parking brake and apply the throttle slowly and smoothly.,['Manual34_111', 'Manual34_24', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

When placing the V-belt into the holder, make sure it is seated correctly and then tighten the retaining bolt securely. A loose bolt may allow the belt to shift or fall out during operation.

<PIC>

Also check that the fuel hose is routed properly and is not pinched when the V-belt is installed in the holder. The belt and holder should not press against or trap the hose.

<PIC>

Important: the spare V-belt must be secured firmly in the holder. If it comes loose, it may contact hot components such as the muffler, which could damage the belt and make it unusable when needed.

Before riding, make sure the steering system is working correctly. As part of your pre-operation check, inspect the throttle, brake, and steering before starting the engine. For the steering system specifically, check for excessive looseness or play at the handlebars, steering column, and skis. If anything feels loose, abnormal, or does not move smoothly, do not operate the snowmobile until it has been inspected and corrected.

<PIC>

When operating the snowmobile, always keep both hands on the handlebars and keep your feet on the running boards. Do not place your feet outside the running boards while riding. To steer in normal riding, turn the handlebars in the direction you want to go.

For most snow surfaces, proper body positioning is very important. As you approach a turn:

1. Slow down before entering the curve.
2. Begin turning the handlebars in the desired direction.
3. Shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn.
4. Lean your upper body into the turn to help maintain balance and control.

<PIC>

Practice this turning technique at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. Avoid higher speeds until you are fully familiar with how the snowmobile responds to steering, throttle, braking, and body movement.

Please also keep these safety points in mind: abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movement, or entering a turn too fast can cause the snowmobile to tip. If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn to help stabilize the machine.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

For most snow surfaces, the key to turning a snowmobile is using “body English”—shifting your body weight together with the handlebar input to help the machine stay balanced and follow the curve.

1. **Slow down before the curve.** As you approach the turn, reduce speed early and smoothly. Avoid entering the turn too fast.
2. **Turn the handlebars in the direction you want to go.** Begin steering gradually rather than making a sudden movement.
3. **Shift your weight to the inside of the turn.** Put more weight on the running board on the inside of the curve.
4. **Lean your upper body into the turn.** Your body position helps counterbalance the snowmobile and improves control.

<PIC>

5. **Practice at low speed first.** Repeat this technique many times in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles until it feels natural.
6. **Increase difficulty only after you are comfortable.** Once you have learned the technique, you can use it at higher speeds or in tighter curves. The sharper or faster the turn, the more you will need to lean into it.

If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, **lean farther into the turn** to help regain balance. If needed, **gradually ease off the throttle** and/or **steer slightly toward the outside of the turn**—avoid sudden braking or abrupt throttle changes.

Remember: do not ride at higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with your snowmobile’s handling and controls.,['Manual34_127']

Here are the proper techniques for riding uphill on a snowmobile:

1. Start with gentle slopes first  
Practice on small, gradual hills before attempting steeper or more difficult climbs. Uphill riding requires good throttle control, balance, and the ability to shift your body weight correctly.

2. Build momentum before the climb  
As you approach the hill, accelerate before you actually start climbing. Once the snowmobile begins going uphill, reduce the throttle slightly and keep it smooth. This helps prevent the track from spinning or losing traction.

3. Keep your body weight on the uphill side  
Maintaining the correct riding position is very important. When climbing straight up a hill, lean forward to keep weight over the skis and improve control. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars.

<PIC>

The image shows the rider shifting body weight forward and uphill, which helps the snowmobile stay balanced and maintain traction while climbing.

4. Slow down near the crest  
As you reach the top of the hill, reduce speed and be ready to react. There may be obstacles, a sharp drop, another rider, or a vehicle on the other side.

5. If you cannot continue climbing, do not spin the track  
If the snowmobile loses momentum and cannot make it up the hill, avoid applying more throttle just to spin the track. Instead:
- Stop the engine.
- Set the parking brake.
- Pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back down the hill.
- Stay on the uphill side of the machine; do not stand or move to the downhill side.
- Once the snowmobile is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

Important safety note: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobile riders. Build skill gradually before attempting difficult uphill terrain.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, focus on keeping the machine controlled rather than letting it coast freely.

1. Keep your speed low
   Begin the descent slowly and maintain minimum speed throughout the downhill section. Avoid sudden acceleration or sharp steering inputs.

2. Keep the clutch engaged
   Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while going downhill. This helps the engine compression slow the snowmobile, preventing it from free-rolling down the slope.

3. Use light, frequent braking
   Apply the brake often, but only with light pressure. Gentle braking helps manage speed without causing the track to skid.

<PIC>

4. Maintain a stable riding position
   Stay balanced and prepared to adjust your body position as the slope changes. Keep both hands on the handlebars and remain alert for bumps, obstacles, or changes in snow conditions.

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking during a descent. Excessive braking can lock the track, which may cause loss of control. Always brake gradually and avoid sudden or hard brake application.,['Manual34_128']

When crossing a slope on a snowmobile, use extra caution and focus on balance, body position, and smooth steering. Side-hill riding is not recommended for beginners or novice riders, because maintaining control on a slope requires experience and quick weight adjustment.

Key techniques:

1. Shift your weight uphill
As you travel across the face of the slope, lean your body toward the uphill side. This helps counteract the pull of gravity and keeps the snowmobile more stable.

2. Use the recommended side-hill riding position
A stable position is to place the knee of your downhill leg on the seat, while keeping the foot of your uphill leg on the uphill running board. This position makes it easier to move your body weight as needed while crossing the slope.

<PIC>

3. Be prepared for sideways sliding
Snow and ice can cause the snowmobile to slide sideways down the slope. If this happens and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. Once the machine regains balance and traction, gradually steer back toward your intended direction of travel.

4. If the snowmobile begins to tip
If the snowmobile starts tipping, steer down the hill to help regain balance. Avoid sudden steering, braking, or throttle changes, as these can make the machine harder to control.

5. If you cannot recover balance
If you cannot maintain correct balance and the snowmobile is going to tip over, get off immediately on the uphill side. This helps keep you away from the machine if it rolls or slides downhill.,['Manual34_129']

Here are the recommended steps to inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile:

1. **Let the engine cool before inspection.**  
   If the engine has been running, the engine and muffler can be very hot. Avoid touching them with your body or clothing until they have cooled down.

2. **Remove the spark plug and inspect it periodically.**  
   Spark plugs gradually wear because heat and deposits can cause the electrodes to erode and the plug to break down over time.

3. **Check the porcelain insulator color.**  
   Look at the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. For normal riding conditions, the ideal color is **medium to light tan**.

4. **Watch for abnormal coloring.**  
   If the spark plug color is clearly different from normal, it may indicate an engine-related issue. For example, a very white center electrode porcelain area may point to an intake air leak or carburetion problem for that cylinder. In this case, it is best to have the snowmobile checked by a dealer rather than trying to diagnose the issue yourself.

5. **Measure and adjust the electrode gap.**  
   Before reinstalling the spark plug, use a wire thickness gauge to measure the electrode gap and adjust it to the specified value if needed. In the image below, item **1** shows the spark plug reach/thread length, and item **2** shows the electrode gap to be checked.

   <PIC>

6. **Confirm the spark plug has the correct reach.**  
   Always use a spark plug with the proper thread length, also called the reach. If the reach is too long, it can cause overheating and engine damage. If it is too short, it may cause spark plug fouling, poor performance, carbon buildup on exposed threads, hot spots in the combustion chamber, and possible thread damage.

   <PIC>

7. **Clean the plug and mounting surfaces before installation.**  
   Before installing the spark plug, clean the gasket surface and wipe any grime or deposits from the threads.

8. **Install and tighten to the proper torque.**  
   Tighten the spark plug correctly to avoid poor sealing or thread damage.

   **Spark plug torque:** 28 Nm / 2.8 m·kg / 20 ft·lb

If the plug is heavily carboned, wet, damaged, or the engine has poor spark, misfires, or low power, clean or replace the spark plug as needed and have the snowmobile checked if the problem continues.,['Manual34_139', 'Manual34_138']

To clean a snowmobile properly, follow these steps:

1. **Clean the entire machine thoroughly**  
   Wash both the outside and the inner areas where dirt, snow residue, corrosive salts, or acidic buildup may collect. Pay extra attention to suspension areas, underbody sections, vents, and places where slush or road grime can accumulate.

2. **Loosen heavy mud, grease, and grime**  
   Use **Mud and Grease Release** or an equivalent cleaner to break down stubborn dirt and oily residue before washing. This helps prevent scratching and makes the wash more effective.

3. **Wash with mild soap**  
   Use a mild soap solution and clean water. Avoid harsh chemicals that may damage painted surfaces, plastics, rubber parts, or metal finishes.

4. **Rinse completely**  
   Rinse all cleaned areas thoroughly to remove soap, cleaner, salt, and loosened grime. Make sure no detergent remains in tight spaces or around metal parts.

5. **Dry completely**  
   Dry the snowmobile fully after rinsing. Removing moisture helps reduce the chance of corrosion, especially on metal components and suspension surfaces.

**Important note:** If the snowmobile was transported through areas where road salt is used, clean it as soon as you reach your destination. Road salt is highly corrosive and should be removed promptly to help protect the machine.
